<!-- DOC10 plan (now shipped — preserved for slice context).

**DOC10 — README Contributing + License footer `_TODO` resolution (DoD #12 polish step; MISSION §10 "README — user-facing docs"):** smallest end-to-end slice that moves DoD #12 closer. After DOC9a, three `_TODO` blocks remain in `README.md` outside the Common-workflows region: line 14 (`_TODO: deployed URL_` in Quick links — gated on P4), line 47 (`_TODO (Fly.io or Render)_` in Tech stack — gated on P4), the entire `## Deployment` section at line 390 (gated on P4), plus the **Contributing** placeholder at line 415 + the **License** placeholder at line 419. **DOC10 closes the two footer `_TODO`s that are not gated on un-shipped state** (Contributing + License — neither depends on P4; both can be filled now from the existing source-of-truth state in `MISSION.md` + `pyproject.toml`). **No DoD ticked.** DoD #12 still needs the deploy section + the deploy-target line + the deployed-URL link, all gated on P4. After DOC10 the only remaining README `_TODO`s are the three deploy-related ones — every other placeholder will be resolved.

- **Scope** (`README.md` + extension of `tests/integration/test_readme.py` only — pure docs slice):
  - **README §`## Contributing`** (replace the single `_TODO_` line with ~150-200 words): names the project as a Claude Code autonomous-build-loop project (anchored on the existing `## How this gets built` section just above + on `MISSION.md` §10's "Files this loop maintains" + on `PROGRESS.md`'s "How to use this file" preamble); explains that the loop is the primary author + a human reviewer ticks DoD items + `MISSION.md` is the only place to propose scope changes; lists the three human-in-the-loop touch-points already mentioned at line 406-409 of the existing README; states explicitly that *external pull requests are not the workflow* (this is an internal UC build, not an open-source project); points a curious reader at `MISSION.md` §8 ("How to work") + the loop log at `loop.sh`. The section closes by saying "if you have feedback or have spotted a bug, open an issue" (mirrors line 5's existing "Open an issue" copy). ~10-15 lines of markdown replacing 1 line of `_TODO_`.
  - **README §`## License`** (replace the single `_TODO_` line with ~50-100 words): names the licence as **Proprietary** to match the canonical declaration in `pyproject.toml` line 7 (`license = { text = "Proprietary" }`). One paragraph explaining: this is an internal UC tool not open-sourced; the source is hosted only for UC + its build loop; no rights are granted to outside parties; for licensing enquiries contact UC. The README §License is the user-facing docs surface for `pyproject.toml`'s `license` metadata; both must agree (same docs↔source consistency posture as DOC9a's PDF-library check vs. `pyproject.toml`).
  - **Why "Proprietary" and not MIT/Apache** (the candidate the Next-slice prose floated): `pyproject.toml` already declares `license = { text = "Proprietary" }` — this is a load-bearing legal field. Silently swapping it to MIT or Apache would either (a) silently change the project's licence (illegal scope change without authorization), or (b) leave the README and `pyproject.toml` disagreeing on the licence — both worse than matching the existing declaration. MISSION.md doesn't say "open source" anywhere; the project is described as UC's internal inventory tool. The conservative move is to match the existing `pyproject.toml` declaration. Logged under "Proposed scope changes" if the human reviewer wants the licence flipped to open-source; the README should track the canonical metadata, not anticipate a change.
  - **No edits to lines 14, 47, 390** — those remain `_TODO` until P4 (deploy decision + `fly.toml` / `render.yaml` config) lands. Touching them silently would either expand DOC10's scope or fictionalise un-shipped state. Kept exactly as-is, same posture as DOC9a's deferral of the same three lines.

- **Test extension** `tests/integration/test_readme.py`: new `TestContributingAndLicenseFooter` class (~50 LoC, **8 forcing-function tests**, mirrors DOC9a's `TestQuickLinksAndTechStackTodoResolution` two-pin shape):
  - `test_contributing_section_is_filled` — `_h2_section("Contributing")` does not contain `_TODO_` AND has body length > 200 chars (mirrors DOC1-DOC8's same-named test). Catches a future PR that overwrites the section back to `_TODO_`.
  - `test_contributing_section_references_mission_and_progress` — body contains both `MISSION.md` AND `PROGRESS.md`. The section's whole purpose is to point the curious reader at the loop's two source-of-truth files.
  - `test_contributing_section_names_loop_driven_posture` — body contains case-insensitive `loop` substring. Pins the project's "this is a Claude Code autonomous build loop" identity so a future PR that softens the prose to "this is a normal repo" fails. Mirrors DOC4's `test_section_explains_archived_item_resolves` posture.
  - `test_contributing_section_says_external_prs_are_not_workflow` — body contains case-insensitive `pull request` OR `external` substring (one-of two — a future PR that explains the no-external-PRs invariant in either of the two natural phrasings still passes). Forces the section to call out the no-strangers-with-PRs convention.
  - `test_license_section_is_filled` — `_h2_section("License")` does not contain `_TODO_` AND has body length > 50 chars.
  - `test_license_section_names_proprietary` — body contains case-sensitive `Proprietary` substring. Case-sensitive on the canonical capitalisation that matches `pyproject.toml`'s declaration.
  - `test_license_matches_pyproject` — opens `pyproject.toml` from disk + asserts `license = { text = "Proprietary" }` substring is present (case-sensitive). Same docs↔source consistency posture as DOC9a's `test_pdf_choice_matches_pyproject`. Forces a docs update on a future swap of `pyproject.toml`'s licence field.
  - `test_no_silent_open_source_license_in_readme` — body does NOT contain case-insensitive `MIT`, `Apache`, `GPL`, or `BSD` substrings (a 4-way absence pin). Forces a future PR that swaps the licence to open-source to *either* update `pyproject.toml` (in which case `test_license_matches_pyproject` still passes only if the README+pyproject still agree) *or* update PROGRESS.md "Proposed scope changes" + `MISSION.md` first.

- **Helper reuse**: `_h2_section(heading: str) -> str` (already added in DOC9a at line 36) sliced both Quick links and Tech stack; DOC10 reuses the same helper for `## Contributing` and `## License`. No new helpers needed.

- **Why this shape, not a full deploy section now**: DOC9 (deploy section in `## Deployment` + the deploy-target line in Tech stack + the deployed-URL link in Quick links) is gated on P4 — actual deploy decision (Fly.io vs Render) + a working `fly.toml` / `render.yaml` + production env handling. Documenting un-shipped state would violate DoD #12's "written for someone who has never seen the project" posture (DOC3 + DOC4 + DOC9a's same lesson). DOC10 lifts the *non-gated* footer placeholders without taking on P4-shaped scope.

- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before writing prose** — same DOC4-DOC9a discipline:
  - `pyproject.toml` line 7 is `license = { text = "Proprietary" }` (verified by reading the file). The README section must match; pinned by `test_license_matches_pyproject`.

- **Scope** (`README.md` two-line edit + extension of `tests/integration/test_readme.py` only — pure docs + tests slice):

Same `<title> — <one-line description>` shape; same relative-path link style.

Names the choice + the rationale anchor + the source-file pointer so a future reader knows where to look.

Same docs ↔ source consistency-pinning posture as A2's `test_audit_coverage.py` cross-cutting source-text sweep.

After P4 ships, DOC9-deploy fills the remaining three deploy-related `_TODO`s; after DOC10 the Contributing + License footer is filled.

- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before committing.** Carried forward DOC4-DOC8's "did I just promise something that doesn't exist?" lesson:
  - `CHANGELOG.md` exists at repo root (verified by `ls`); first line is `# Changelog`; latest entry is `AC1` under `## Unreleased` (matches the iter-58 entry the user would expect to see).
  - `pyproject.toml` line 32 is `"reportlab>=4.5.0",` (verified by `grep -i 'weasyprint|reportlab' pyproject.toml`); no `weasyprint` substring anywhere in `pyproject.toml`. The choice is unambiguous.

- **DoD impact**: **No DoD ticked.** DoD #12 needs every piece (deploy section + Contributing + License + every workflow filled, all of which DOC1-DOC8 + DOC9a now cover except the three deploy-section legs gated on P4 + the two footer placeholders gated on DOC10). DOC9a is the single forcing-function step that lifts every `_TODO` placeholder that doesn't depend on un-shipped state — leaves the README in a state where every remaining `_TODO` is genuinely waiting on a future slice rather than an unupdated copy.

**OAuth1-DC — Drift-catcher integration tests pinning `_dev-login` ↔ `google_callback` parity (DoD #1 evidence-shoring step; MISSION §3 "Auth and roles" + §7 DoD #1):** smallest end-to-end slice that moves DoD #1 closer. The queued OAuth-stub Playwright walk has a technical flaw: Playwright's `context.route` only intercepts browser-issued requests, so it cannot intercept the server-side `oauth.google.authorize_access_token(request)` call inside `google_callback` — that's a `httpx`-level call from FastAPI to Google's token endpoint. A genuine real-route Playwright walk requires server-side Authlib stubbing (~150-200 LoC across a stub module + state/nonce echo + `id_token` JWT signing); too large for one iteration. **OAuth1-DC** is the smaller forcing-function step that pins the structural invariant *both routes share `upsert_user_from_userinfo`*, which is what justifies treating the existing `tests/e2e/test_admin_users_e2e.py` walk via `_dev-login` as legitimate evidence for DoD #1's user-creation + pending-state + role-assignment chain. **Does not tick DoD #1** — the real-Google leg still needs either a server-side OAuth stub slice or a human-driven sanity check with a real GCP project.

- **Scope** (`tests/integration/test_auth_routes.py` only — pure tests-only addition): adds a new `TestDevLoginAndCallbackParity` class at the bottom of the file with 4 forcing-function tests. **No `app/` changes.** mypy source-file count stays at 29.
  - `test_both_routes_call_upsert_user_from_userinfo` — source-text inspection via `inspect.getsource` of `auth_module.google_callback` and `auth_module.dev_login`; both must contain `upsert_user_from_userinfo(`. Catches a future PR that diverges either route from the shared user-upsert helper. Same posture as A2's `test_audit_coverage.py` cross-cutting source-text sweep.
  - `test_callback_and_dev_login_produce_equivalent_user_row` — drives both routes with the same userinfo `{"sub": "drift-1", "email": "drift@example.com", "name": "Drift Tester"}`. Path A: POST `/auth/_dev-login` with that data, fetch the resulting User by `google_sub`, snapshot (email, name, role, status). Reset DB state. Path B: monkeypatch `auth_module.oauth.create_client` to return an `AsyncMock` whose `authorize_access_token` returns `{"userinfo": <same payload>}`, GET `/auth/google/callback`, fetch the User, snapshot the same fields. Assert both snapshots are identical (`email == "drift@example.com"`, `name == "Drift Tester"`, `role is None`, `status is UserStatus.PENDING`). Same posture as the existing `TestGoogleCallback::test_callback_creates_user_and_redirects_to_root` test, but cross-checks against `_dev-login`'s output side-by-side.
  - `test_callback_and_dev_login_produce_equivalent_audit_row` — runs both paths and asserts each produces exactly one `audit_log` row with `action="user.created"`, `entity_type="user"`, `entity_id == user.id`, `before_json IS NULL`, and an `after_json` dict with the same `email` field. Pins the audit-side parity that A2's coverage sweep alone doesn't pin (A2 only pins that *some* `record_audit` call happens, not that the shape matches).
  - `test_callback_and_dev_login_set_session_user_id` — drives each path inside its own `TestClient` (so cookie state doesn't leak between them) and asserts that an immediate follow-up GET `/auth/me` returns the created user's identity for each path. This is a behavioural pin — both routes must set `request.session["user_id"]` to the user's id (otherwise the user appears anonymous after sign-in). Captures the second leg of `google_callback`'s post-userinfo work (after `upsert_user_from_userinfo`, set the cookie + redirect).

- **Why this shape, not a full Playwright walk**: the existing `TestGoogleCallback` integration tests (2 tests) already cover `google_callback` with mocked Authlib. The existing `TestAuthMe` + `TestIndexPage` + `test_admin_users_e2e.py` tests cover `_dev-login` end-to-end. What was missing was a single forcing function that **pins the equivalence** of the two paths so a future PR can't silently drift one without the other. After OAuth1-DC, the test suite says: "if `_dev-login` works, the real OAuth callback works the same way for the same userinfo payload" — a property the e2e walks transitively rely on.

- **Why this doesn't tick DoD #1**: DoD #1 says "A new user can sign in with Google, land in pending state, and an Admin can assign them a role. They can then access the appropriate parts of the app." The phrase "with Google" is the unticked leg — the real OAuth round-trip with Google's authz + token + userinfo endpoints. OAuth1-DC pins the post-Google-userinfo path (which the existing tests already cover) but does not exercise the actual Authlib network calls to `accounts.google.com`. To tick #1, either: (a) a future slice adds a server-side OAuth stub that re-registers the google client with local stub URLs (`/auth/_test-stub/authorize` + `/token` + `/userinfo`) so a Playwright walk can drive `/auth/google/login` → real callback → real Authlib state validation → upsert chain end-to-end without external network; or (b) a human runs the flow against a real GCP test project (out of scope for the loop). OAuth1-DC is the small, low-risk progress step before either of those.

- **Manual sanity check** (no human required): the integration tests *are* the manual sanity check — they drive the actual route handlers with TestClient + monkeypatch + an isolated SQLite session, and the assertions pin the exact equivalence claim. Run as part of `make test` which is part of `make check`.

- **DoD impact**: **No DoD ticked.** OAuth1-DC closes the test-side equivalence-pinning gap that has existed since `_dev-login` was first added in F2. Remaining for DoD #1: server-side OAuth stub + Playwright walk (queued for a future iteration), or a human-driven sanity check.

Remaining DoD #12 work: P4 → DOC9-deploy section → small DOC10-contributing/license footer.
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before committing.** Carried forward DOC4-DOC7's "did I just promise something that doesn't exist?" lesson. Specifically verified: route `GET /admin/audit` at `app/audit_routes.py:89`; role gate `Depends(require_role(Role.MANAGER))` at `:94` + admin blanket pass via `app/auth.py`; `PAGE_SIZE = 50` at `:36`; CSV branch + headers list at `:39-48` + `:105-125`; over-page renders empty (no 404) at `:127-141`; nav-link Manager+Admin visibility gate at `app/templates/base.html:175-183`; six-column header (Time / Actor / Action / Entity / Before / After) at `app/templates/admin_audit.html:25-30`; em-dash for null before/after at `:45-48`; pagination Previous/Next at `:60-65`; immutability triggers via `apply_immutability_triggers` at `app/audit.py:128`; canonical wire-name action strings via `grep -rEn 'action="(item|stock_movement|purchase_order|stock_take|checkout)\.'` (`item.created` at `app/items.py:1162`, `stock_movement.in` at `app/movements.py:366`, `purchase_order.received` at `app/purchase_orders.py:1264`, `stock_take.committed` at `app/stock_takes.py:1121`, `checkout.created` at `app/checkouts.py:343`); 11 distinct `entity_type` values across `app/` (item / item_unit / location / supplier / taxonomy_node / taxonomy_field_def / user / stock_movement / stock_take / checkout / purchase_order); `record_audit` typed signature at `app/audit.py:48-77` requires `actor: User | None`; `audit_log.created_at` server-defaulted via `func.now()` at `app/models.py:1208-1210`. The cross-cutting audit-coverage sweep at `tests/integration/test_audit_coverage.py` enforces `record_audit(...)` is called on every POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE route in `app.routes`. Forcing-function tests don't catch fictional surfaces by themselves — only reading the section against actual app source while writing it does.
- **First-run pass on all 8 forcing-function tests.** No iteration needed (unlike DOC7 where the `/receive` substring was missing from the first draft and the test caught it). Suggests the cross-checking discipline before writing prose is now stable; DOC8's plan was specific enough about every load-bearing token that the first draft hit each forcing-function pin.
- **Section length 3.4 KiB body** — comfortably above the 400-char floor. The audit-trail surface has a role-gate paragraph + a cross-cutting-guarantee paragraph + a 4-step browse walk + a no-item-filter limitation + a CSV-export workaround + a wire-name vocabulary list + an immutability paragraph. Compressing further would drop either the action-vocabulary list (load-bearing for a Manager who wants to grep the CSV) or the no-item-filter prose (load-bearing for a Manager who scans the page in vain for a filter form). The vocabulary list takes ~10 lines but is the single biggest grep-target in the docs and is the only docs-side mention of `entity_type` so a developer reading the README knows where to look in the CSV.
- **`Manager` and `Admin` are pinned together by `test_section_names_manager_and_admin_roles`.** This is different from DOC5/DOC6/DOC7 where Office was named first. DoD #12 doesn't name the audit-trail role; the gate at `app/audit_routes.py:94` is `Depends(require_role(Role.MANAGER))` which means Admin always passes (via `app/auth.py`'s blanket override) and Manager passes — Office and Workshop both 403. The README must name both allowed roles so the section is honest about who can read it; pinning both halves catches a future PR that accidentally narrows the role gate to just `Role.ADMIN` or just `Role.MANAGER` without updating the docs.
- **`/admin/audit?format=csv` literal path pinned by `test_section_names_csv_export`** rather than just `?format=csv`. The literal lock catches a future rename of the route prefix or query param. AC1's CSV branch is the only documented bulk-export surface for the audit log and the second-most-likely path a Manager hunting an item's history will take (after Cmd+F).
- **Four column labels pinned by `test_section_names_audit_columns`.** `Time` / `Actor` / `Action` are case-sensitive (the literal column-header strings rendered by `admin_audit.html:25-27`); `before` / `after` are case-insensitive (they appear both as column labels and as JSON-dict prose). The `Entity` column is implicit in the `entity_type:entity_id` discussion but isn't pinned by name in the test set — the column order is locked by the four-of-six labels we DO pin, so a future rename of `Entity` to `Target` would still pass this test. Acceptable: the column-header label rename is a tiny risk and over-pinning every word in the table is brittle.
- **`append-only` + `cannot be edited` both pinned by `test_section_names_immutability_invariant`.** MISSION §9 has the hardest rule for this surface ("Do not delete the audit log. Do not provide a way to edit it"). The README must name both halves (the *append-only* invariant + the *can't-be-edited* invariant) so a future PR that softens the docs to "the audit log is mostly read-only" or "the audit log is normally append-only" fails this test. Trade-off: tight string matching on user-facing prose is brittle; acceptable since this is a load-bearing security/integrity invariant.
- **Sample of canonical wire-names pinned by `test_section_names_canonical_audit_actions`** rather than the full vocabulary. Pinned: `item.created` + `stock_movement.in` + `purchase_order.received` + `stock_take.committed` (one per top-level domain — items / movements / POs / stock takes). The README also lists `checkout.created/returned` and the `supplier`/`location`/`taxonomy_*`/`user`/`item_unit` entity-types but these aren't pinned by the test set: pinning every wire-name would make the test brittle without adding force (a future rename of `item.created` to `item.create` is the failure mode that matters most because that's the most-likely typo). Same posture as DOC5's audit-trail-link pin without naming individual `stock_take.*` actions.
- **No-item-filter limitation pinned by `test_section_explains_no_item_filter_limitation`** — case-insensitive `filter` + case-sensitive `CSV` + at-least-one-of-three keyboard-search tokens (`cmd+f` / `ctrl+f` / `cmd-f`). This is a load-bearing v1 limitation that's easy to silently remove from the docs once A1b lands a real filter form. Same posture as DOC4's `test_section_explains_archived_item_resolves` — a workflow nuance that needs a forcing function so it can't get silently softened. Trade-off: the keyboard token disjunction is brittle to future rephrasing (e.g. "use your browser's search"); acceptable since the three tokens cover macOS + Windows + a hyphenated variant. If a future PR replaces "Cmd+F" with "press Find", this test fails — at which point the section either gets a "Cmd+F" reference back or the test relaxes to just `filter` + `CSV`.
- **`(system)` actor noted but not pinned by a test.** The README mentions `(system)` for background events (bootstrap-admin promotion). A future PR that drops the `(system)` mention would still pass the test set but would silently confuse a reader seeing `(system)` rendered for the first time. Acceptable: the template literal at `admin_audit.html:38` (`{% else %}(system)`) makes the rendering observable; over-pinning prose-flavoured details would calcify the docs. If a future iteration's reader finds the omission confusing, queue a forcing function then.
- **No e2e walk added.** Same posture as DOC1-DOC7. The on-disk file read is the right granularity for a docs artefact. The existing `test_audit_view_routes.py` (~20+ tests) covers Manager + Admin → `/admin/audit` → paginated table render → CSV branch end-to-end, and the cross-cutting `test_audit_coverage.py` enforces every mutation route writes an audit row, so the README walk-through is verifiably accurate against current behaviour.
- **Forcing-function shape closes at 8 sections × 5-8 tests each** (5 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 8 + 8 = 56 tests in `test_readme.py`). The `_section()` helper has now been reused 56 times; the slicer-to-next-`### `-or-`## ` heading approach is stable. After DOC9 (deploy) + DOC10 (contributing/license) the test file caps out at ~75-80 tests — small enough to keep in one module without splitting.
- **Maintainability**: ~80 new LoC for the `TestReadingAuditTrailSection` class. A competent dev can read + extend it in 5 min using DOC7's section as a template. The forcing-function shape is now a stable template across 8 sections.
- **Engine isolation preserved.** No writers, no models, no router registration changes, no audit-trail behaviour edits. Pure README + test addition. mypy source-file count stays at 29.
- **Iter-66 count anchor was stale by 19** — re-anchored this iteration. iter-66 PROGRESS.md "Current state" claimed `2386 unit+integration ✓` and rationalised "iteration 65's PROGRESS.md claimed 2394 but the on-disk `.loop_logs/check_65.log` shows 2378, so iter-64-baseline was correctly 2371 + DOC6's 7 = 2378; DOC7 brings the true total to 2386." But running `git stash && uv run pytest -q --collect-only` against iter-66's HEAD (`53e7f89`) reveals 2405 tests collected (2402 passed + 3 skipped). The +16-19 hidden tests iter-66 dismissed as fictitious actually were real — they exist in the test files at iter-66's HEAD. iter-66's check_66.log shows `2386 passed, 3 skipped` but that line doesn't reflect on-disk state at HEAD `53e7f89`. **Forward-going lesson: anchor counts to `pytest -q | tail -1` against the actual on-disk HEAD before committing, never to PROGRESS.md prose recollection of prior counts.** The lesson was already stated in iter-66 self-critique; iter-67 is the first iteration that actually applied it — and turned up the real anchor.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 source files unchanged), pytest 2410 passed + 3 skipped (was 2402 passed + 3 skipped at the iter-66 HEAD; **+8 DOC8 forcing-function tests**), e2e 16 passed (no count change; pure docs slice).

-->

---

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — DOC7:**

- **No DoD tick.** DOC7 is the seventh of ~7-8 docs slices needed before DoD #12 can tick. One remaining workflow section (DOC8 — audit trail), plus the deploy section (gated on P4), plus contributing + license. After DOC7 every "Common workflows" subsection except `### Reading the audit trail for an item` is filled; the audit-trail walk-through is the natural next slice and builds directly on the cross-cutting wiring that A1 + A2 + AC1 already shipped.
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before committing.** Carried forward DOC4-DOC6's "did I just promise something that doesn't exist?" lesson. Specifically verified: the receivable statuses tuple `(SENT, PARTIALLY_RECEIVED)` at `app/purchase_orders.py:1059-1062`; the role gate `Depends(require_role(Role.MANAGER, Role.OFFICE))` at `:1114`/`:1141` plus Admin's blanket pass via `app/auth.py`; the receive-form route paths `GET /admin/purchase-orders/{po_id}/receive` (`:1110`) and `POST .../receive` (`:1137`); the per-line form-field naming convention `received_<line_id>` + `cost_<line_id>` at `:1177-1180`; the over-receipt rejection `qty_received + new_received > qty_ordered → 400` at `:1185-1193`; the all-zero no-op flash _"PO #N — no receipts entered."_ at `:1248-1249`; the FIFO-cost-layer source `CostLayerSource.PO_RECEIPT` at `:1233`; the cumulative-state status flip rule (every line `qty_received >= qty_ordered` → `RECEIVED`, else `PARTIALLY_RECEIVED`) at `:1257-1258`; the `purchase_order.received` audit shape with `before={"status": prev_status.value}` + `after={"status": new_status.value, "lines": [{line_id, received_qty, actual_unit_cost, movement_id}, ...]}` at `:1261-1272`; the receive-button visibility gate via `is_receivable` at `purchase_order_detail.html:116`; the form column labels (SKU / Name / Unit / Qty ordered / Already received / Outstanding / Receiving now / Actual unit cost) at `purchase_order_receive_form.html:25-34`. Forcing-function tests don't catch fictional surfaces by themselves — only reading the section against actual app source while writing it does.
- **First run failed on the `/receive` substring assertion** — the README walk-through described the **Receive against this PO** button click but never the literal URL path `/receive`. The test caught it (exactly the forcing-function purpose). Fix: added "(or visit `/admin/purchase-orders/{id}/receive` directly)" parenthetical to step 3, mirroring DOC4's `/scan/item/{id}` direct-link pattern. A user debugging the form load can now bookmark the URL — small UX win on top of the test pass.
- **Plan miscounted "~7 forcing-function tests" but shipped 8.** The plan's enumerated list named 8 tests; the rounded "~7" descriptor was sloppy. Logged in the slice's "Tests" line below the plan as 8 — the truth in the count anchor matters. Recorded "Test count drift correction" lesson: enumerate, then count, before writing prose. (Same kind of count drift caught in DOC6 self-critique against iteration 64's PROGRESS — happens at the prose layer, not the code layer.)
- **Section length 2.5 KiB body** — comfortably above the 400-char floor. The receive flow has a status-gate prerequisite + a form-table column list + two per-line input semantics + a status-flip rule + an over-receipt rejection + an all-zero no-op; compressing further would drop either the over-receipt prose (load-bearing for a confused operator who tries to record extra stock) or the all-zero no-op (load-bearing for a user who hits **Record receipt** without filling any line and wonders why nothing changed).
- **`Office` named first per MISSION §3.103 + DoD #6.** Manager + Admin appear as also-allowed in the same opening sentence. Pinned by `test_section_names_office_role`. Same posture as DOC5 (stock takes) + DOC6 (PO send).
- **Receivable statuses pinned by `test_section_names_receivable_statuses`** with `sent` case-insensitive + `partially_received` case-sensitive. The case-sensitive pin on the multi-word status catches a future rename to `partial` / `partial_received` (most likely typo trajectory). The case-insensitive `sent` tolerates sentence-start capitalisations.
- **`partially_received` substring overlap between `test_section_names_receivable_statuses` and `test_section_names_partial_vs_full_status_flip` is intentional.** The first pins the status as a *precondition* for receipt; the second pins it as an *outcome* of receipt. Both invariants need their own forcing-function. A future PR that drops `partially_received` from the docs would fail both tests with two distinct error messages — the granularity is worth the substring redundancy. (Same posture as DOC4's two-URL pin on `/scan` + `/scan/item/{id}`.)
- **FIFO cost-layer creation pinned by `test_section_names_fifo_cost_layer_creation`** with `FIFO` case-sensitive + `cost layer` case-insensitive. `FIFO` is the canonical acronym across MISSION + cost-engine source + every prior README reference (DOC5's stock-take walk + DOC6's PO-send walk both use uppercase `FIFO`). A future PR that demotes the acronym to `fifo` or removes the cost-layer-creation copy entirely would fail.
- **Expected-vs-actual cost distinction pinned by `test_section_names_actual_vs_expected_cost`** mirroring DOC6's same-named test exactly. This is the load-bearing FIFO-engine wiring concept; DOC6 documents the *write* side (expected on the PO line is what gets emailed) and DOC7 documents the *receive* side (actual is what creates the cost layer). Both halves of the docs need to consistently name both halves of the concept.
- **No mention of `purchase_order.received` audit-action wire-name in the test set.** The audit-coverage sweep in `test_audit_coverage.py` already pins that the route writes the row (cross-cutting integration test), and per-route tests in `test_purchase_orders_routes.py` pin the action name (runtime assertion). A README forcing-function on the wire-name would over-pin a brittle string the user doesn't actually see in the UI; the cross-link to `/admin/audit` is enough. Same posture as DOC5's audit-trail link without naming individual `stock_take.*` actions.
- **No e2e walk added.** Same posture as DOC1-DOC6. The on-disk file read is the right granularity for a docs artefact. The existing `test_purchase_orders_routes.py` (~150+ tests) covers Office/Manager → receive form → submit → status flip end-to-end across role + status-guard + over-receipt + atomic-validation + all-zero-no-op + audit-shape + FIFO-cost-layer matrices, so the README walk-through is verifiably accurate against current behaviour.
- **Forcing-function shape now established at 7 sections × 5-8 tests each.** DOC8 will follow the same template — section-is-filled, references-route, names-role, plus 1-3 section-specific markers. The `_section()` helper continues to be reused; the audit-trail section will likely pin `/admin/audit` + the `actor + timestamp + before/after` audit-row vocabulary + the cross-link from item detail.
- **`_section()` helper edge case observed but not blocking.** Same as DOC6 — the receive section uses numbered lists + paragraphs without inner `####` markdown headings, so the slicer terminates at the next `### ` (`### Reading the audit trail for an item`) cleanly.
- **Maintainability**: ~70 new LoC for the `TestReceivingStockAgainstPOSection` class. A competent dev can read + extend it in 5 min using DOC6's section as a template. The forcing-function shape is now a stable template across 7 sections.
- **Engine isolation preserved.** No writers, no models, no router registration changes, no audit-trail edits. Pure README + test addition.
- **Iteration-65 e2e flake notes** (carried forward from the resumption note in the plan): `test_office_reads_dashboard_with_low_stock_widget`'s second `_admin_promote` call timed out at the default Playwright 30s on the failing run; a re-run inside the full e2e suite passes in ~21s. The failure point was `_dev_login` → `page.wait_for_url(f"{base_url}/")` after the admin's second sign-in. Possible mitigation if it recurs: explicit `wait_for_url(..., timeout=60_000)` on the `_dev_login` calls, or pre-creating the admin user via a conftest fixture so the second sign-in skips the upsert path. **Defer.** Single-flake threshold is one occurrence; MISSION §8 stuck-detection threshold is three iterations in a row. If the same test flakes again next iteration, queue an explicit hardening slice (~10 LoC).
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 source files unchanged), pytest 2386 (+8 from a corrected baseline of 2378 — see "Test count anchor reset" below), e2e 16 (no count change; pure docs slice).
- **Test count anchor reset.** Iteration 65's PROGRESS.md "Current state" line claimed `pytest 2394 unit+integration ✓ (+7 DOC6 forcing-function tests)` and rationalised "the 2371 figure recorded in iteration 64 understated the true running total — actual prior was 2387, so DOC6 brings it to 2394." But the on-disk `.loop_logs/check_65.log` shows `2378 passed, 3 skipped` — meaning the iter-64-baseline was 2371 (matches the iter-64 log) and DOC6's `+7` brought it to 2378 cleanly. There was no hidden +16 sub-iteration sweep. Iteration 65 invented an extra +16 in prose. This iteration's count of 2386 = 2378 (true iter-65 baseline) + 8 (DOC7) confirms 2378 was correct. Refreshing "Current state" now to 2386, dropping the false 2394 anchor. Forward-going lesson: anchor counts to the actual `.loop_logs/check_<n>.log` line, not to recollection.

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — DOC6:**

- **No DoD tick.** DOC6 is the sixth of ~7-8 docs slices needed before DoD #12 can tick. Two remaining workflow sections (PO receive — DOC7, audit trail — DOC8) plus the deploy section (gated on P4) plus contributing + license. After DOC6 the supplier + category + item + scan + stock-take + PO-send hex is documented; the remaining workflows (PO receive + audit trail) build directly on DOC6's PO scaffolding.
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before committing.** Carried forward DOC4 / DOC5's "did I just promise something that doesn't exist?" lesson. Specifically verified: the five PO statuses against `app/models.py:932-936` (`POStatus.DRAFT/SENT/PARTIALLY_RECEIVED/RECEIVED/CANCELLED`); the dual-router structure (`draft_router` at `/admin/reorder` + `list_router` at `/admin/purchase-orders`) at `app/purchase_orders.py:112-115`; the role gate `Depends(require_role(Role.MANAGER, Role.OFFICE))` at lines `:221`/`:340`/`:414` plus Admin pass-through; the five audit actions (`purchase_order.created`/`updated`/`sent`/`cancelled`/`received`) at `:269`/`:689`/`:975`/`:729`/`:1264`; the PDF link visibility gate `po.status.value != "cancelled"` in `purchase_order_detail.html:170`; the no-supplier-email blocker prose copy `"This supplier has no email address — add one on the supplier record before sending."` at `:99`; the no-supplier-on-item blocker `"Assign an active supplier on the item before drafting a PO."` at `reorder_dashboard.html:19`; the supplier-archived blocker `"Supplier is archived — unarchive it or move items to an active supplier first."` at `:23`; the cancel button copy `"Cancel this draft"` at `:108`; the **Send to supplier** button copy at `:95`; the **Save changes** button copy at `:86`; the **Draft PO from this supplier** button copy at `reorder_dashboard.html:33`; the four edit-form fields (Expected date / Notes / per-line Qty ordered / per-line Expected unit cost) at `:24-74`; the CSV-list-link `/admin/purchase-orders?format=csv&status_filter=...` at `purchase_orders_list.html:18`. Forcing-function tests don't catch fictional surfaces by themselves — only reading the section against actual app source while writing it does.
- **Section length 2.9 KiB body** — comfortably above the 400-char floor. POs have a five-status lifecycle + a draft-edit form + a PDF + an email send + a cancel branch + an audit-action quintuple; compressing further would drop either the cost-distinction paragraph (load-bearing for FIFO understanding) or the blocker prose (load-bearing for the user staring at a button-less detail page wondering why).
- **`Office` named first per MISSION §3.103 + DoD #6.** Manager + Admin appear as also-allowed in the second sentence. Pinned by `test_section_names_office_role`.
- **Five PO statuses pinned by `test_section_names_po_statuses`.** `partially_received` is the only multi-word status (with an underscore) — pinned case-sensitively to catch a future rename to `partial` / `partial_received`. The other four are case-insensitive substrings to tolerate sentence-start capitalisations. A future PR that drops a status from the lifecycle paragraph fails the test on first run.
- **Expected-vs-actual cost distinction pinned by `test_section_names_expected_vs_actual_cost`** with both phrases case-insensitive. This is the load-bearing FIFO-engine wiring concept; a section that names only "expected" or only "actual" silently confuses readers about what the PO line cost actually does. Trade-off: tight string matching on user-facing prose is brittle; acceptable since the README is supposed to track the public-vocabulary distinction.
- **`PDF` substring is case-sensitive.** PDF is an acronym — always uppercase. The forcing function catches a future PR that demotes "PDF" to "pdf" or removes the link entirely.
- **`email` substring is case-insensitive** — matches both prose ("emails the PO") and the configurable `EMAIL_BACKEND` env-var reference.
- **`/admin/reorder` and `/admin/purchase-orders` are pinned as separate URLs.** The two routers serve different purposes (drafting from low-stock vs. browsing/editing the resulting POs); a future PR that drops either reference fails on a separate test for clear failure-message granularity. Same posture as DOC4's `/scan` + `/scan/item/{id}` two-URL pin.
- **No mention of "Receive against this PO" in DOC6.** The receive flow is DOC7's slice — including it here would either duplicate content (when DOC7 lands) or create a silent merge-window where the PO-receive walk lives in DOC6's section. The closing paragraph names the `partially_received` and `received` statuses but defers the actual flow to DOC7.
- **No mention of the `?format=csv` parameter** beyond a single-sentence reference to **Download CSV** on the list view. R5 / R5b's CSV story has its own cross-cutting concern; documenting every `?format=csv` query param across every list view would balloon the section. The forward-pointer is enough for a user who notices the link.
- **No e2e walk added.** Same posture as DOC1-DOC5. The on-disk file read is the right granularity for a docs artefact. The existing `test_reorder_e2e.py` walk re-runs green and independently verifies the underlying reorder→draft chain end-to-end.
- **Forcing-function shape now established at 6 sections × 5-7 tests each.** DOC7-DOC8 will follow the same template — section-is-filled, references-route, names-role, plus 1-3 section-specific markers per section. The `_section()` helper continues to be reused.
- **`_section()` helper edge case observed but not blocking.** The helper slices to the first of the next `### ` or `## ` marker. In DOC6's section there's no markdown heading inside the body (numbered lists + paragraphs only), so the slicer's behaviour is correct. If a future docs slice adds a `#### ` deep nesting inside a workflow body, the slicer keeps it inside the section (correct).
- **Maintainability**: ~70 new LoC for the `TestGeneratingPurchaseOrderSection` class. A competent dev can read + extend it in 5 min using DOC5's section as a template. The forcing-function shape is now a stable template across 6 sections.
- **Engine isolation preserved.** No writers, no models, no router registration changes, no audit-trail edits. Pure README + test addition.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 files unchanged), pytest 2394 (+7), e2e re-checked `test_reorder_e2e.py` ✓.
- **Test count drift caught + corrected.** PROGRESS.md from iteration 64 stated 2371 as the prior count; the actual current count after running the full suite is 2387 (without my changes; 2394 with). Adjusted in the Tests: line. This kind of drift happens when a prior iteration adds tests via a sub-slice or refactor that doesn't re-anchor the count; documented for future-DOC slices to do a `pytest -q | tail -1` reality-check before committing the count.

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — DOC5:**

- **No DoD tick.** DOC5 is the fifth of ~7-8 docs slices needed before DoD #12 can tick. Three remaining workflow sections (PO send, PO receive, audit trail) plus the deploy section (which needs P4 to land first) plus contributing + license. After DOC5, every DoD-named workflow ("how to do a stock take") plus the supplier + item lead-ins are documented; the remaining workflow slices (DOC6 + DOC7 + DOC8) are PO + audit-trail-specific and don't block any ticked DoD's docs coverage.
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before committing.** Carried forward the "did I just promise something that doesn't exist?" lesson from DOC4 (which itself was a forward-port of DOC3's first-draft fictional `/admin/items/{id}/qr` reference). Specifically verified: the four audit actions (`stock_take.created` / `started` / `counted` / `committed`) all exist at `app/stock_takes.py:738`/`:844`/`:953`/`:1121`; the start-time snapshot pulls `system_qty=item.current_qty` (`app/stock_takes.py:829`); the negative-variance atomic rollback re-renders with `commit_form_values=raw_unit_costs` preserved (`app/stock_takes.py:1083-1093`); the `data-direction` attr on the commit row distinguishes increase/decrease (`stock_take_detail.html:190`); the **All items** / **Category** / **Location** scope vocabulary matches the radio labels on the form (`stock_take_form.html:23/31/48`); the role gate is `require_role(Role.MANAGER, Role.OFFICE)` with Admin pass-through (`app/stock_takes.py:674`/`:703` + `app/auth.py:164`); the `stock_take_id` FK on `StockMovement` is the cross-link the audit view uses (`app/stock_takes.py:1051`). Forcing-function tests don't catch fictional surfaces by themselves — only reading the section against actual app source while writing it does.
- **Section length 2.7 KiB body** — comfortably above the 400-char floor. Stock takes have a four-stage lifecycle + an FIFO-engine semantic + an atomic-rollback story; compressing further would drop the rollback paragraph (the most surprising part for a future operator who's never seen it before).
- **`Office` named first per MISSION §3.103 + DoD #5.** Manager + Admin appear as also-allowed in the second sentence. Pinned by `test_section_names_office_role` — a future PR that drops Office from the docs (or relegates it to Manager-primary) fails the suite.
- **Scope vocabulary pinned by the user-facing labels (`Category` / `Location` / `All items`), not the wire values (`node` / `location` / `all`).** README readers see the radio button labels, not the form-encoded enum values. A future PR that renames a label fails the test and forces a docs update on the same PR. Trade-off: zero false-positives at the cost of label-rename brittleness; acceptable since the README must track the user-facing strings.
- **`FIFO` substring is case-sensitive.** The acronym is the canonical spelling across MISSION (§3 + §6) + every cost-engine module + the seven prior README references. A lowercase `fifo` would be wrong copy.
- **`audit` substring is case-insensitive** — matches both prose ("audit trail") and the `/admin/audit` route reference. A future PR that drops the audit-trail link section fails the test.
- **`variance` + `commit` substrings case-insensitive** — both appear in multiple forms (`variance`, `Variance`, `commit`, `Commit count`, `committing`). The forcing-function pins the load-bearing concept rather than a specific noun-vs-verb form.
- **No e2e walk added.** Same posture as DOC1-DOC4. The on-disk file read is the right granularity for a docs artefact. The existing `test_stock_takes_e2e.py` walk independently verifies the underlying stock-take flow end-to-end (re-run this iteration as a sanity check — green).
- **Forcing-function shape now established at 5 sections × 5-7 tests each.** DOC6-DOC8 will follow the same template — section-is-filled, references-route, names-role, plus 1-3 section-specific markers per section. The `_section()` helper continues to be reused.
- **No mention of the "ST cancel route" backlog item.** The README documents only what's shipped (per DOC4's lesson). The walk-through ends at the commit branch + the atomic-rollback retry; if the operator wants to abandon a stock take without committing, today they leave it `in_progress` and start a fresh one. Adding a fictional **Cancel** button would violate DoD #12's "written for someone who has never seen the project" posture; if ST cancel ships, the section should add a paragraph (and the forcing-function suite should pin the new copy).
- **No mention of CSV export on the stock-takes list.** R5c's `/admin/stock-takes?format=csv` link is on the list page (`stock_takes_list.html:29`); the README walk-through stays focused on running a stock take rather than every list-page affordance. If a future "Bulk reporting" section lands, the CSV link gets covered there instead of bloating this section.
- **Maintainability**: ~70 new LoC for the `TestRunningAStockTakeSection` class. A competent dev can read + extend it in 5 min using DOC4's section as a template.
- **Engine isolation preserved.** No writers, no models, no router registration changes. Pure README + test addition.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 files unchanged), pytest 2371 (was 2364, +7), e2e re-checked `test_stock_takes_e2e.py` ✓.

---

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — DOC4:**

- **No DoD tick.** DOC4 is the fourth of ~7-8 docs slices needed before DoD #12 can tick. Three remaining workflow sections (stock take, PO send, PO receive, audit trail) plus the deploy section (which needs P4 to land first). After DOC4 the supplier + category + item + scan quartet is documented — every Workshop-facing surface is now in the README.
- **Caught a non-existent surface during planning.** The section heading says "Printing a QR label" but I4 (QR label generation, `/admin/items/{id}/qr`) is still in the Backlog. First instinct was to either skip the section or document a fictional `/admin/items/{id}/qr` route. Neither is acceptable: skipping leaves the `_TODO` in place (no DoD progress); documenting a fictional surface violates DoD #12's "written for someone who has never seen the project" posture. The compromise: the section keeps the heading (matches the `_TODO` placeholder + the eventual I4 expansion point) but the body is honest about today's state — items carry a `QR code` string typed during creation; print labels by hand; the printable-label view lands in a future slice. A future I4 slice will then expand the printing paragraph in place.
- **Forcing-function tests don't catch fictional URLs by themselves.** The only defence against documenting a non-existent surface is reading the section against the actual app source while writing it (same lesson as DOC3, where the first draft promised a non-existent QR auto-generation surface). Worth carrying forward as a self-critique entry: future docs slices need a "did I just promise a route that doesn't exist?" cross-check.
- **Section length comfortably above the 300-char floor** (~1.7 KiB body). Lower floor than DOC3's 400 because the section is genuinely shorter — there's no field-by-field walk; it's mostly a usage flow. Compressing further would lose the conceptual scaffolding (USB vs camera, QR-vs-SKU precedence, archived-resolves posture) the rest of the doc relies on.
- **Two `/scan` URL substring tests, not one.** `test_section_references_scan_route` pins `/scan` (catches the landing page) and `test_section_references_scan_item_action_picker_route` pins `/scan/item/{id}` (catches the action picker URL). A future PR that drops the action-picker reference would still pass the first test but fail the second. Trade-off: two tests vs one with both URLs as substrings; pick two for the more granular failure message.
- **`QR` and `SKU` as case-sensitive substrings** matches the README copy. The third sub-assertion (`qr_code` lowercase OR `QR code`) tolerates either the implementation-detail name or the user-facing label — doesn't force one wording. If a future PR wants to drop `qr_code` from prose entirely, the test still passes if `QR code` is present.
- **`USB` case-sensitive vs `camera` case-insensitive.** USB is an acronym — always uppercase in the section; locking case is a free constraint. `camera` appears both lowercase (in prose) and as part of "Use camera" (button label, capital U) — case-insensitive matches both.
- **`archived` case-insensitive matches both the prose ("archived items still resolve") and the badge label ("archived" lowercase in `scan-resolved-archived-badge`).** A future PR that swaps to "Archived" capital A would still pass.
- **`/scan/item/{id}` literal vs `/scan/item/`.** Used the placeholder form `{id}` so the docs match the conceptual URL, not a fictional concrete id. The forcing-function test pins the literal `{id}` substring — catches future drift to `<item_id>` or `/scan/item/`.
- **No e2e walk added.** Same posture as DOC1 + DOC2 + DOC3. The on-disk file read is the right granularity for a docs artefact. The existing `test_scan_e2e.py` walk independently verifies the underlying scan flow end-to-end.
- **Forcing-function shape now established at 4 sections × 5-7 tests each.** DOC5..DOC8 will follow the same template. The `_section()` helper continues to be reused.
- **Pre-existing DOC1+DOC2+DOC3 self-critiques still hold.** The `### ` boundary detection caveat is unchanged; not an issue for DOC4 since the section has no code blocks.
- **Maintainability**: ~70 new LoC for the `TestPrintingQrAndScanningSection` class. A competent dev can read + extend it in 5 min using DOC3's section as a template.
- **Engine isolation preserved.** No writers, no models, no router registration changes. Pure README + test addition.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 files unchanged), pytest 2364 (was 2357, +7), e2e 16 (no count change).

<!-- DOC3 plan (now shipped) — kept commented for audit-trail context only.

**DOC3 — README "Creating an item" workflow walk-through (DoD #12 polish step; MISSION §10 "README — user-facing docs"):** smallest end-to-end slice that moves DoD #12 closer. After DOC3 the item-creation leg of DoD #12's "how to add a supplier and an item" is done; subsequent slices fill the remaining sections (QR/scan, stock take, PO send, PO receive, audit trail).

- **Scope** (`README.md` only — pure docs slice + extension of `tests/integration/test_readme.py`): replaced the `_TODO` block under `### Creating an item` with a concrete walk-through anchored on the actual UI (Manager role, `/admin/items`, **New item** button, the four required core fields SKU+Name+Category+Unit, the `qty` vs `unique` tracking modes + the `Manage units` follow-up for unique items, the `requires_checkout` flag for tools/moulds, the Manager-only reorder fields, the optional supplier/location/QR/notes, the custom-field fieldset rendered from the leaf node's schema (per DOC2), and the Archive/Unarchive posture). ~30 lines of markdown replacing 1 line of `_TODO`.
- **Test extension** `tests/integration/test_readme.py`: new `TestCreatingAnItemSection` class (~7 forcing-function tests) reading `README.md` from disk: section is filled (no `_TODO`, len > 400); references `/admin/items`; names the `Manager` role; names the four required core fields (SKU, Name, Category, Unit, plus required keyword); names `qty` + `unique` tracking modes (catches a future model rename); names the `check-out` / `check out` concept (case-insensitive); explains both `Archive` and `Unarchive`.

-->

---

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — DOC3:**

- **No DoD tick.** DOC3 is the third of ~7-8 docs slices needed before DoD #12 can tick. Four remaining workflow sections (QR/scan, stock take, PO send, PO receive, audit trail) plus the deploy section (which needs P4 to land first). After DOC3 the supplier + category + item triumvirate is documented — the rest of the workflows reference items, so DOC4..DOC8 are unblocked.
- **Caught a forward-looking promise during self-review.** First draft said "QR code — leave blank to auto-generate via the QR label view" — but I4 (QR label generation) is still in the Backlog and that surface doesn't exist. Fixed before commit: now reads "type any unique string, or leave blank for items that don't carry a label". README must describe what's shipped, not what's planned, otherwise DoD #12's "written for someone who has never seen the project" is violated. Forcing-function tests don't catch promises about non-existent routes; the only defence is reading the section against the actual app source while writing it.
- **Section length comfortably above the 400-char floor** (~2 KiB body, the longest workflow section so far). Items have many fields — four required core, two reorder, four optional, plus the tracking-mode and requires-checkout flags, plus inherited custom fields. Compressing further would lose the conceptual scaffolding (e.g. why `current_qty` is read-only, why archived items can't be hard-deleted) that the rest of the doc relies on.
- **`len > 400` floor matches DOC2.** Items warrant the same depth as categories. A future PR that drops below 400 chars is almost certainly a regression.
- **Tracking-mode vocabulary pinned by `qty` + `unique` substring matches.** If `app/models.py::TrackingMode` renames a value (e.g. `qty` → `bulk` or `unique` → `serial`), the test fails — forcing the docs to update alongside the model. Same forcing-function shape as DOC2's `select`/`multiselect` test.
- **Required-fields test pins the four current required core fields by name** (`SKU`, `Name`, `Category`, `Unit`). Brittle to label renames — but those four labels have been stable since I1a, and a rename is exactly the kind of UI change that *should* require a docs update on the same PR.
- **`requires_checkout` test pins the concept (case-insensitive `check-out` or `check out`).** Not the field key itself (`requires_checkout` is too implementation-detail-y for a user-facing README). This catches both "Requires check-out" (the form label) and "check out" (prose mentions). DoD #4 (check-out flow) depends on this concept being introduced, so the docs must keep it.
- **`Archive` / `Unarchive` substring match is case-sensitive** — same posture as DOC1 + DOC2. Capital-A matches the button labels exactly.
- **No e2e walk added.** Same posture as DOC1 + DOC2. The on-disk file read is the right granularity for a docs artefact.
- **Forcing-function shape now established at 3 sections × 5-7 tests each.** DOC4..DOC8 will follow the same template — section-is-filled, references-route, names-role, plus 1-3 section-specific markers per section. The `_section()` helper continues to be reused.
- **Pre-existing DOC1+DOC2 self-critiques still hold.** The `### ` boundary detection caveat (a `### ` inside a code block would mis-cut) is unchanged; not an issue for DOC3 since the section has no code blocks.
- **Maintainability**: ~50 new LoC for the `TestCreatingAnItemSection` class. A competent dev can read + extend it in 5 min using DOC2's section as a template.
- **Engine isolation preserved.** No writers, no models, no router registration changes. Pure README + test addition.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 files unchanged), pytest 2357 (was 2350, +7), e2e 16 (no count change).

After DOC1 the supplier-add bit of DoD #12's "how to add a supplier and an item" is done; subsequent slices fill the remaining sections.

- **Scope** (`README.md` only — pure docs slice + 1 small integration-test module): replaced the `_TODO` block under `### Adding a new supplier` (line 154) with a concrete numbered walk-through anchored on the actual UI (Manager role, `/admin/suppliers`, the four form fields name/email/phone/notes, archive vs. delete posture). ~16 lines of markdown replacing 1 line of `_TODO`.
- **New tests-only module** `tests/integration/test_readme.py` (~60 LoC): 5 forcing-function tests under `TestAddingANewSupplierSection` reading `README.md` from disk: section is filled (no `_TODO`); references `/admin/suppliers`; names the `Manager` role; names the required `Name` field; explains both `Archive` and `Unarchive`.

-->

---

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — DOC2:**

- **No DoD tick.** DOC2 is the second of ~7-8 docs slices needed before DoD #12 can tick. Five remaining workflow sections (item, scan/QR, stock take, PO send, PO receive, audit trail) plus the deploy section (which needs P4 to land first). After DOC2 the supplier + category prerequisites are documented; DOC3 (item) is the next prerequisite for almost every other workflow.
- **Section length comfortably above the 400-char floor** (~1.4 KiB body). Categories warrant more depth than suppliers because of the leaf-node concept + the schema-versioning posture — a workshop user reading this needs to understand why fields can only attach to leaves, why archived nodes still appear on historical items, and why fields can't be hard-deleted. Compressing further would lose the conceptual scaffolding the rest of the doc relies on.
- **`len > 400` floor (vs. DOC1's `> 200`)** is calibrated to the section's actual content. The category section has more conceptual scaffolding than the supplier section (leaf-node rule, schema versioning, three Archive surfaces). 400 is a soft guard against accidental truncation, not a target.
- **Field type vocabulary pinned by `select` + `multiselect` substring matches.** If `app/models.py::FieldType` renames a value (e.g. `multiselect` → `multi_select`), the test fails — forcing the docs to update alongside the model. Trade-off: zero false-positives at the cost of model-rename brittleness; acceptable since the README is supposed to track the public API. Could pin every type (`text/number/decimal/date/boolean/select/multiselect`) but the marginal coverage isn't worth the noise on a renamed type.
- **Leaf-node concept pinned by `leaf` (case-insensitive) + `Sub-category` / `sub-category` substring.** The leaf-node rule is load-bearing — a future PR that drops the rule from the section would silently confuse readers about where fields attach. The substring match catches both the explicit "leaf node" wording and "leaf" used in surrounding prose.
- **No URL change risk for now.** `/admin/taxonomy` has been stable since S3; the test is unlikely to false-fail on URL drift alone. If the route gets renamed in a future slice, the test fails immediately and the README needs updating in the same PR — same forcing function as DOC1.
- **`Archive` / `Unarchive` substring match is case-sensitive** — same posture as DOC1. Capital-A matches the button labels on `/admin/taxonomy` and `/admin/taxonomy/{id}/fields` exactly.
- **No e2e walk added.** The README is a docs artefact, not a UI surface. The on-disk file read is the right granularity. Same posture as DOC1.
- **Forcing-function shape now established** at 2 sections × 5-7 tests each. Each future section gets its own `TestSection` class with: section-is-filled (`_TODO_` absent + soft length floor); references-route; names-role; 1-2 section-specific markers (field type, FIFO concept, PO status, etc.). Pattern is identical to RBAC1's per-route role tests vs. cross-cutting boundary check.
- **Maintainability**: extension to existing `test_readme.py` (~50 new LoC for the class). A competent dev can read + extend it in 5 min using DOC1's section as a template. The `_section()` helper is already there.
- **Engine isolation preserved.** No writers, no models, no router registration changes. Pure README + test addition.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 files unchanged), pytest 2350 (was 2343, +7), e2e 16 (no count change).

---

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — DOC1:**

- **No DoD tick.** DOC1 is one of ~7-8 docs slices needed before DoD #12 can tick. Each remaining workflow section is its own slice (item, stock take, PO send, PO receive, audit trail, QR/scan label) plus the deploy section (which needs P4 to land first). Cumulative cost ~6-8 iterations; trade-off is queueing them one at a time means a future revert can't undo more than one section's work.
- **Forcing-function test posture matches RBAC1/A2 precedent.** Each future workflow slice gets its own `TestSection` class in `test_readme.py` with the same shape: section-is-filled, references-route, names-role, plus 1-2 section-specific markers. Same separation as RBAC1's per-route role tests vs. cross-cutting boundary check.
- **Section length feels short** but the supplier flow really is that simple — name + 3 optional fields + archive/unarchive. A longer section would be padding. Compare against the existing "Configuring Google SSO" section (4 numbered steps + a paragraph) which is similar size. If a future user reports the section is unclear, expand on demand.
- **`### ` vs. `## ` boundary detection.** The `_section()` helper slices to the *first* of the next `### ` or `## ` marker. Catches both same-level siblings ("Defining a new category…") and ancestor-level transitions ("## Project layout"). If a future README adds a `#### ` deep-nested heading inside a workflow body, it stays inside the section (correct). If a future README puts a `### ` inside a code block (rare but possible), the slicer would prematurely cut — would need a markdown-aware parser. v1 lift not justified; queued in self-critique.
- **No URL change risk for now.** `/admin/suppliers` has been stable since S1; the test is unlikely to false-fail on URL drift alone. If the route gets renamed in a future slice, the test fails immediately and the README needs updating in the same PR — that's the forcing function working as designed.
- **`Archive` / `Unarchive` substring match is case-sensitive.** Capital-A matches the button label exactly. If a future slice renames the button, the test fails — same forcing function. Trade-off: zero false-positives at the cost of brittleness against UI label changes; acceptable since the README is supposed to track UI labels.
- **No e2e walk added.** The README is a docs artefact, not a UI surface. The on-disk file read is the right granularity. e2e would re-test what the integration tests already pin from a higher cost. Same posture as every other CSV-link forcing-function test.
- **`len(body.strip()) > 200`** is a soft guard against accidental truncation — currently the body is ~700 chars. If a future PR reduces it below 200, the test fails. Picked 200 over 400 to leave room for future sections that might be shorter (e.g. "Reading the audit trail" might be a single paragraph).
- **`_README` resolves at module import** rather than per-test. Cheap; the file is small. If the file is missing on disk (impossible on a normal checkout) the first test's `_section()` call fails with a clear `FileNotFoundError`.
- **Maintainability**: ~60 LoC test module; a competent dev can read + extend it (add a new `TestSection` for the next workflow) in 5 min. Pattern is identical to AC1's CSV-link tests in shape.
- **Engine isolation preserved.** No writers, no models, no router registration changes. Pure README + test addition.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 files unchanged), pytest 2343 (was 2338, +5), e2e 16 (no count change).

<!-- AC1 plan (now shipped) — kept commented for audit-trail context only.

**AC1 — CSV export on `/admin/audit` (audit-view CSV polish; MISSION §3 "Export any list view to CSV"):** smallest mechanical end-to-end slice. Extends `app/audit_routes.py::list_audit` with a `?format=csv` query branch via the existing `csv_branch` helper, and adds an `audit-csv-link` "Download CSV" link to `admin_audit.html`. Doesn't tick a DoD — completes the established CSV-export pattern across every list view (eighth+ surface; the ninth if checkouts admin from R5h is counted), specifically queued from the "Next slice" candidate list under "Audit view CSV export — apply the existing csv_branch pattern to /admin/audit?format=csv. ~10 LoC + 4 tests."

- **Route extension** (`app/audit_routes.py::list_audit`):
  - New `format: str = ""` query param. `format == "csv"` triggers CSV via `csv_branch`; anything else (blank / `html` / garbage) renders HTML — same silent-coerce posture as `show` on locations / suppliers / taxonomy.
  - **Role gate inherited**: existing `Depends(require_role(Role.MANAGER))` already blocks Workshop + Office + pending at the dependency layer (Admin always passes via `require_role`). No inner role-split needed; same posture as the locations / taxonomy / variance-trend CSV — Office isn't on the audit view at all (audit is Manager-owned per A1's design, MISSION §3 "Audit and history").
  - **CSV branch ignores pagination.** A CSV export is a snapshot artefact; a paginated CSV would force the receiver to fetch + concatenate pages themselves, defeating the purpose. The CSV branch fetches *all* rows ordered newest-first via the same `LEFT JOIN User` shape, then streams them. No `LIMIT` / `OFFSET` — same posture as the supplier / location / taxonomy CSVs (none of which are paginated either). For v1 audit volumes (a few thousand rows / year), the unbounded fetch is fine; cursor-based pagination is queued under A1's self-critique if volumes grow.
  - **Headers** (8 columns): `id`, `created_at`, `actor_email`, `action`, `entity_type`, `entity_id`, `before_json`, `after_json`. Mirrors the HTML table's six visual columns plus two explicit ID columns: `id` (HTML carries it as `data-audit-id`, not a cell — making it explicit lets a downstream consumer reference rows) and `actor_email` (replaces the HTML's `actor.email` / `(system)` rendering with a plain email or empty cell — `(system)` would mis-sort against actual emails in a spreadsheet).
  - **`actor_email` cell**: actor's email when present, empty when `actor_id IS NULL` (system action). Empty cell is correct for spreadsheet receivers — same posture as `unit_serial` / `holder_email` on the checkouts admin CSV (R5h).
  - **`before_json` / `after_json` cells**: rendered as **compact JSON** via `json.dumps(value, separators=(",", ":"), default=str)`, matching the HTML's `tojson` filter shape. `None` renders as empty string (via `csv_response`'s coercion). Without the `default=str` fallback a non-JSON-serialisable dict value (e.g. a `Decimal` that escaped `_to_jsonable`) would crash the export — defensive but cheap.
  - **Filename**: `audit_log.csv`. No filter state in the filename (the audit view doesn't have show / status / scope filters yet — A1b will add them; if A1b lands first, the filename will need to thread the filter state in like `purchase_orders_{status}.csv`).
  - Module-level `_AUDIT_CSV_HEADERS` constant + new `_csv_rows_for_audit(rows)` helper. Mirrors the seven prior surfaces' precedent.
  - **Return type widened** on `list_audit` from `HTMLResponse` to `Response`; `@router.get("", response_class=HTMLResponse)` decorator simplified to `@router.get("")`. The `HTMLResponse` import is removed from the module — no other route in the module uses it.
  - **Walrus form**: `if (resp := csv_branch(format, filename=..., headers=..., rows=...)) is not None: return resp` — same shape as the seven prior sites.
  - **Sort survives CSV**: same `ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC` as the HTML branch — newest first.

- **Template** (`admin_audit.html`): new `<a data-testid="audit-csv-link" href="/admin/audit?format=csv">Download CSV</a>` link block sitting between the summary line and the table. No filter state to preserve in the href in this slice.

- **Role enforcement preserved**: anon hits the dependency and 401s before reaching the format check. Pending / Workshop / Office hit the same dependency and 403.

- **Read-only invariant preserved**: GET-CSV writes no audit row. Pinned by `TestAuditCsvReadOnly::test_csv_writes_no_audit`.

- **Manual sanity check** (no human required): the integration tests POST end-to-end through the CSV branch and assert (a) the role gate fires before the format check (anon 401 + Workshop/Office/pending 403), (b) the response carries the right `Content-Type` + `Content-Disposition` filename, (c) the body is an RFC-4180 CSV with the right headers + cells, (d) JSON cells round-trip through `json.loads`, (e) system actions render with empty `actor_email`, (f) the export ignores pagination (60-row seed → 60 data rows in the CSV vs. 50 rows on page 1 of the HTML), (g) blank / unknown `format` renders HTML.

- **Tests** (18 new in `tests/integration/test_audit_view_routes.py`):
  - `TestAuditCsvRoleEnforcement` (6): anon CSV 401; pending CSV 403; Workshop CSV 403; Office CSV 403; Manager CSV 200; Admin CSV 200.
  - `TestAuditCsvHeaders` (2): Content-Type carries `text/csv; charset=utf-8`; filename `audit_log.csv` in `Content-Disposition`.
  - `TestAuditCsvBody` (5): empty emits header-only; one row produces one data row with right cells (action + actor email); system actor renders empty actor_email cell; `before_json` / `after_json` round-trip via `json.loads`; `null` `before_json` renders as empty string (vs. the HTML's em-dash).
  - `TestAuditCsvSnapshot` (1): 60 seeded rows produce 60 data rows (export ignores pagination — vs. 50 visible on page 1 of the HTML).
  - `TestAuditCsvHtmlBranch` (2): blank format renders HTML; unknown format `?format=garbage` renders HTML.
  - `TestAuditCsvReadOnly` (1): GET-CSV writes no audit row (compares pre/post audit row counts).
  - `TestAuditCsvLink` (1): HTML page renders the `audit-csv-link` link.

**Repo health (post-AC1, verified)**:
- ruff: clean (`app tests`)
- mypy: clean (strict on `app/`, 29 source files unchanged)
- pytest unit + integration: 2338 passing (was 2320, +18)
- Playwright e2e: 16 passing (no count change)

**DoD impact**: no DoD ticks. AC1 is feature polish that completes the CSV-export pattern across every list view in the system. The remaining unticked DoDs (#1, #10, #11, #12) are unaffected by AC1 directly but the pattern continuity makes the eventual "every list view exports CSV" assertion in the README (DoD #12) trivially true.

-->

---

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — AC1:**

- **No DoD tick.** AC1 is polish — completes the established CSV-export pattern across every list view in the system. The unticked DoDs (#1, #10, #11, #12) require larger slices (OAuth Playwright stub walk, conftest Postgres switch, README walk-throughs). AC1's contribution is removing one item from the polish backlog so future iterations can focus on DoD-ticking work.

- **Unbounded fetch on the CSV branch.** The CSV does `SELECT * FROM audit_log ORDER BY created_at DESC` with no LIMIT. For v1 install volumes (a few thousand rows / year) this is fast on SQLite + Postgres (the existing `ix_audit_log_created_at` index makes the sort cheap). Tens of thousands of rows would take a few hundred ms to materialise; hundreds of thousands would warrant a streaming `StreamingResponse` over a server-side cursor. Documented; queued for the day audit volumes outgrow the in-memory buffer.

- **CSV-injection risk inherited.** Same posture as every other CSV surface — a value starting with `=`, `+`, `-`, or `@` could be interpreted as a formula by Excel/Sheets. The audit log's `before_json` / `after_json` cells are wrapped in JSON object delimiters (`{"...":"..."}`), which protects them; but `action` / `entity_type` / `actor_email` are direct strings. v1 risk model is all-internal users; mitigation lands when the threat model changes (queued under R5 self-critique).

- **No filter state in the URL.** A1 hasn't shipped filters yet (queued as A1b). When A1b lands, the CSV-link href will need to thread the filter state in (`actor=…&entity_type=…&since=…`) the same way locations / suppliers / taxonomy thread `show=...`. AC1's link is `format=csv` only — the simplest correct shape for the current zero-filter view.

- **JSON cells with embedded commas/quotes.** stdlib `csv.writer` handles the quoting: a cell containing `{"role":"manager"}` becomes `"{""role"":""manager""}"` after RFC-4180 escaping. Round-trip through `json.loads` is verified by `TestAuditCsvBody::test_before_after_round_trip_via_json_loads` — that's the canonical reader test.

- **Pagination ignored is the load-bearing semantic of the CSV branch.** A user who paginated to page 3 and clicked Download CSV gets ALL rows, not page 3. This is the right shape — a CSV export is a snapshot, not a window. Pinned by `TestAuditCsvSnapshot::test_csv_export_ignores_pagination` (60 rows seeded, all 60 in the CSV; vs. 50 on page 1 of the HTML).

- **Why the explicit `if format == "csv"` over the walrus shape.** The seven prior CSV surfaces use `if (resp := csv_branch(...)) is not None: return resp` because their CSV and HTML branches read from the same fetched `rows` list (no per-branch query difference). AC1 needs a *different* query for CSV (no LIMIT/OFFSET) than HTML — so the early-return + assert shape is clearer than a walrus. Same posture would apply to any future CSV branch that needs a different fetch shape than its HTML sibling. Trade-off: 4 LoC of explicit check + assert vs. a uniform walrus pattern; readability wins when the branches diverge.

- **`assert resp is not None` after `csv_branch`.** Type-narrowing for mypy: `csv_branch` returns `Response | None` and we already checked `format == "csv"`. The assert documents the invariant + lets mypy collapse the union. Could also have called `csv_response(...)` directly to skip the helper, but using `csv_branch` keeps the magic string `"csv"` in one place (its single-responsibility — same posture as R5e justified at the helper-extraction time).

- **Defensive `default=str` on `json.dumps`.** Belt-and-braces against a non-JSON-serialisable value escaping `_to_jsonable` at write time. Falls back to `str(value)` for the offending leaf, keeping the export running rather than 500-ing the request. Acceptable — exports should be lenient where reads can be (a Manager downloading the audit log to investigate an incident shouldn't fail because one historical row had a malformed value). The lenient stringification of an unexpected leaf doesn't *hide* the issue — the cell is still there, just stringified.

- **No e2e walk added.** AC1 is integration-test territory. The CSV branch is a thin wrapper; e2e would re-test what the integration tests pin from a higher cost. No manager-walk extension warranted (the existing audit-view-walk-or-test posture matches every other CSV surface — none have e2e walks for the CSV link itself).

- **Read-only invariant preserved.** `TestAuditCsvReadOnly::test_csv_writes_no_audit` compares pre/post audit row counts. The CSV branch is pure-read.

- **No mypy source-file count change.** AC1 is a pure extension of `app/audit_routes.py`; the file stays mounted in `main.py` as before. mypy strict on `app/` covers 29 source files unchanged.

- **One new test helper** (`_audit_rows`) at module level. Mirrors the helpers in `test_locations_routes.py` etc. for the read-only CSV count check.

- **Pattern continuity.** AC1 is the eighth CSV-export surface (after R5 variance trend + R5 PO list + R5b items list + R5c stock-takes + R5d suppliers + R5e helper extraction + R5f locations + R5g taxonomy + R5h checkouts admin). Following the same pattern means a future ninth surface (movements timeline / users / reorder dashboard / sub-category list) is purely mechanical. The remaining ones are queued under "R5i — CSV export on remaining list views".

- **Maintainability**: ~80 LoC route addition + 3 LoC template change + ~210 LoC tests. Boring beats clever (MISSION §4). Pattern is identical to seven prior surfaces; a competent dev can read + extend it in 10 min.

- **Forcing function for future audit-view changes.** A future PR that breaks the CSV branch (e.g. adds a filter to the HTML query without applying it to the CSV query) would fail `TestAuditCsvSnapshot::test_csv_export_ignores_pagination` if pagination crept back in, or the body-shape tests if columns shifted. The headers + filename pinning catches accidental renames.

- **No nav-link change.** The audit view is still reached via the existing `nav-audit` link in `base.html`. AC1's CSV link is *inside* the audit view, not a nav entry. Same posture as every other CSV link — discoverable from the page, not the global nav.

- **`json` import is module-level**, not lazy. The lazy-import alternative would save ~150ns per request on the HTML branch (which doesn't use `json`). Not worth the complexity. Same posture as every other CSV-export module.

<!-- D7 plan (now shipped) — kept commented for audit-trail context only.

**D7 — Office dashboard non-zero-widgets walk (DoD #7 ticking step; MISSION §3 "Reporting and analytics dashboard"):** smallest end-to-end slice that closes DoD #7's manual-sanity-check leg. The dashboard widgets already exist (R1/R2/R3/R4/R5 shipped; route `/admin/dashboard` aggregates total-value / low-stock-count / overdue-checkouts / open-pos-count / top-consumed / COGS) and integration tests pin each aggregation in `tests/integration/test_dashboard_routes.py`. End-to-end coverage today:

- `dashboard-total-value` — pinned at `175.0000` in `test_manager_dashboard_walk` (populated FIFO).
- `dashboard-low-stock-count` — only pinned at `"0"` (item kept above threshold). **Gap: non-zero path not pinned end-to-end.**
- `dashboard-overdue-checkouts` — pinned at `"1"` in `test_checkouts_e2e.py::test_workshop_checks_out_a_unique_tracked_unit` (populated overdue), then back to `"0"` after check-in.
- `dashboard-open-pos-count` — only pinned at `"0"` (no PO seeded). **Gap: non-zero path not pinned end-to-end** — but ticking this isn't load-bearing for DoD #7 (covered by reorder_e2e walk's PO chain through integration tests).
- `dashboard-top-consumed-row` — pinned populated (`DASH-1`, qty 30); the empty-state placeholder `dashboard-top-consumed-empty` not pinned end-to-end.
- `dashboard-cogs-amount` — pinned at `75.0000` (populated).
- **All e2e walks today sign in as Manager** to read the dashboard. Office user (also allowed by `require_role(MANAGER, OFFICE)`) is verified by RBAC1's gate sweep but not by a render-time walk.

D7 closes the gaps with one sibling walk in `tests/e2e/test_dashboard_e2e.py`: an Office user reads the dashboard with **a single low-stock item active and no movements seeded** — pins low-stock=1, total-value=0, top-consumed-empty placeholder, plus Office-render. After D7, every widget DoD #7 calls out has at least one non-trivial e2e pin (zero or non-zero) somewhere in the suite, and the Office role's dashboard render is verified end-to-end. **DoD #7 ticks this iteration.**

- **Pure tests-only addition** — no `app/` changes; mypy source-file count stays at 29.
