**Self-critique notes (rolling) — CONFTEST2:**

- **No DoD tick.** CONFTEST2 closes (b) of DoD #11's three sub-parts: (a) CONFTEST1's URL/dialect switch ✓, (b) per-test isolation on shared Postgres ✓ (this slice), (c) Postgres CI job (deferred — no remote yet, no `.github/workflows/`). All three must land before DoD #11 ticks. The next natural slice pivots away from DoD #11 toward DoD #1 (OAuth-stub server-side Playwright walk) since (c) is gated on a configured remote.
- **No real Postgres exercise.** The savepoint pattern is exercised only against SQLite-file in tests (with the SQLAlchemy-docs-recommended `connect`/`begin` events to disable pysqlite's automatic transaction handling). An actual Postgres run is the missing piece. Mitigation: the helper is dialect-agnostic at the SQL level — SAVEPOINTs are part of the SQL standard and SQLAlchemy emits identical SAVEPOINT statements on every backend. The SQLite-file test is a faithful surrogate. **A real Postgres smoke run** is a one-line developer check (`createdb test_uc && TEST_DATABASE_URL=postgresql+psycopg:///test_uc make test`) that the CI infra slice (c) will automate.
- **The pysqlite-fix events live in the test fixture, not the helper.** Trade-off: the helper assumes the engine is correctly configured for SAVEPOINT use. On Postgres this is automatic. On SQLite, callers must install the `connect`/`begin` events (the SQLAlchemy-recommended boilerplate from https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/dialects/sqlite.html#serializable-isolation-savepoints-transactional-ddl). This keeps the helper minimal — it's fundamentally a Postgres tool — and lets SQLite-side validation tests use the standard incantation. **Alternative considered + rejected**: install the events inside `_make_isolated_session` itself, gated on `engine.dialect.name == "sqlite"`. Rejected because (a) event listeners accumulate across helper calls (would need de-duplication), (b) adds dialect-specific complexity to a helper that conceptually shouldn't care about dialects, (c) the SQLite fix is itself a SQLAlchemy-docs idiom — keeping it where it's needed (test fixtures) is clearer than embedding it deep in the helper. Logged for the rolling self-critique; revisit if a SQLite production scenario emerges (none is expected per MISSION §5).
- **First-run failures on 3 of 9 tests caught the SQLite quirk early.** The 3 failures (`test_commit_does_not_escape_outer_transaction` + `test_rollback_inside_helper_preserves_outer_transaction` + `test_integrity_error_recovery`) all fired on rows persisting after teardown — the diagnostic content was the right shape (the savepoint pattern itself is correct; the SQLite driver's automatic transaction handling was suppressing the rollback). Diagnosed via a one-line `uv run python -c "..."` repro: `connection.in_transaction()` returned True throughout, but `transaction.rollback()` had no effect on the persisted data. The SQLAlchemy docs' SQLite-fix events resolved it. **Lesson**: when validating a SAVEPOINT-based pattern with SQLite, the pysqlite-fix events from the SQLAlchemy SQLite dialect docs are mandatory boilerplate, not optional polish. Future Postgres-side validation work that uses SQLite as a surrogate must install these events.
- **`Connection` vs `Engine` bind dispatch in `client` fixture is clean.** The `isinstance(bind, Connection)` check is type-safe (SQLAlchemy's `Session.get_bind()` returns whatever was passed to the sessionmaker) and the dispatch is one if/else. A future PR that adds a third bind type (unlikely — SQLAlchemy doesn't define one) would need to extend the dispatcher. Logged.
- **Dispatch on URL prefix uses `startswith("sqlite")`** — same posture as `app/db.py::_engine_kwargs` and CONFTEST1's `_make_test_engine`. A future PR that adds `mssql` or `mysql` URLs would need the dispatch updated alongside the helpers. Tests pin the dispatch via source-text inspection (`test_db_session_dispatch_source_branches_on_url_prefix`) so a refactor that breaks the contract fails the suite.
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before writing tests.** Verified: SQLAlchemy 2.0.49 installed (`Session.__init__` accepts `join_transaction_mode` per `help(Session.__init__)`); 9 unit-test files have local `db` fixtures that hardcode their own engines and are unaffected by CONFTEST2; 30+ `db.rollback()` calls in unit tests are also local — unaffected; `tests/conftest.py:69-103` was the existing `db_session` + `client` fixture pre-edit. The IntegrityError recovery test mirrors the ~30 unit-test commit-then-attempt-duplicate-then-rollback pattern so a future regression in savepoint-recovery semantics fails visibly.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓ (one auto-fix needed: removed unused `Base` import flagged by F401); mypy ✓ (29 source files unchanged — CONFTEST2 is tests-only, mypy strict only on `app/`); pytest 2511 passed + 3 skipped (was 2502 + 3 at iter-75 HEAD `e22ca9f`; **+9 CONFTEST2 tests**); e2e 16/16 passing (full re-run; tests-only refactor doesn't affect the e2e suite which has its own conftest at `tests/e2e/conftest.py`).

---

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — CONFTEST1:**

- **No DoD tick.** CONFTEST1 closes (a) of DoD #11's three sub-parts: (a) conftest URL/dialect switch ✓, (b) Postgres CI job (deferred — no remote yet, no `.github/workflows/`), (c) per-test isolation strategy on shared Postgres backend (deferred — needs transaction-rollback fixtures or schema-per-test). All three must land before DoD #11 ticks. The next natural slice is CONFTEST2 (per-test isolation); CI YAML is gated on a remote being configured first.
- **Per-test isolation gap on shared Postgres is a real limitation.** With the SQLite default, each test creates its own `_make_test_engine(...)` instance + its own in-memory DB → automatic isolation. With `TEST_DATABASE_URL=postgresql+psycopg://...`, every test creates a new engine pointing at the same backing DB, and `Base.metadata.create_all` is idempotent on the schema but row data persists. The first failing-test demo would be: any test that creates a `User` would conflict with a follow-up test's `User` create on `users.email` unique constraint. **Documented in the conftest docstring** + this self-critique note. CONFTEST2 closes the gap with the SQLAlchemy "join the engine to a connection in a transaction, with a SAVEPOINT for nested" pattern (concrete plan in Next slice).
- **`postgresql+psycopg://` driver-scheme catch via first-run failure.** Initial test attempt used the bare `postgresql://` URL scheme which SQLAlchemy maps to psycopg2 by default → `ModuleNotFoundError`. The project ships psycopg v3 (`pyproject.toml:32` is `"psycopg[binary]>=3.2"`), so the URL scheme must be `postgresql+psycopg://...` for SQLAlchemy to pick the right driver. **Lesson logged**: when writing tests that build engines for non-default drivers, anchor the URL scheme to the actual installed driver, not the bare protocol name. Future Postgres docs (e.g. README "running tests against Postgres" section, queued for CONFTEST2) must use the `+psycopg` suffix in example URLs.
- **`TEST_DATABASE_URL` is decoupled from app-side `DATABASE_URL`.** The existing line `os.environ.setdefault("DATABASE_URL", "sqlite:///:memory:")` continues to drive the app config; `TEST_DATABASE_URL` drives only the test fixture's engine. This is intentional — a developer can point only the *fixture* at Postgres without changing the app's runtime DB. For full app/test parity (the more common pattern), they'd set both env vars together. A future README docs slice should clarify the two-env-var pattern. Logged in self-critique; no action this iteration.
- **Helper naming follows app/db.py convention.** Module-level `_resolve_test_database_url()` + `_make_test_engine(url)` are underscore-prefixed (private to the module) but importable by tests via the explicit `from tests.conftest import ...`. Same posture as `app/db.py::_engine_kwargs` which is private-but-importable. The pattern is stable + maintainable.
- **No e2e walk added.** CONFTEST1 is a tests-only refactor; the e2e suite has its own `tests/e2e/conftest.py` that spins up a real uvicorn against a file-backed SQLite (uses `DATABASE_URL`, not `TEST_DATABASE_URL`). Integration tests pin every observable byte of the helper behaviour. Re-running the full e2e suite (16/16 passing) is the regression check that the refactor didn't break anything in the unit + integration → e2e seam.
- **Iter-74 count anchor was stale by 16 — re-anchored this iteration.** iter-74 PROGRESS.md "Current state" claimed `2508 passed + 3 skipped` but on-disk reality at iter-74 HEAD `f452740` is `2492 + 3` (re-anchored via `git stash && git checkout f452740 -- tests/conftest.py && uv run pytest --collect-only`). Same kind of count drift the iter-67 self-critique flagged about iter-66 + the iter-74 self-critique flagged about iter-73. CONFTEST1's true delta is +10 (2492 → 2502), not the +10 implied by the stale anchor (2508 → 2518). **The pattern keeps recurring**: anchor counts to `pytest --collect-only` against the actual on-disk HEAD, never to PROGRESS.md prose recollection of prior counts.
- **First-run pass on 8 of 10 tests; 2 first-run failures both diagnostic.** The 2 failures (`test_postgres_url_yields_postgres_dialect_lazily` + `test_postgres_url_does_not_use_static_pool`) both fired on `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'psycopg2'` — the diagnostic content was the right shape (the helper logic isn't broken; the URL scheme was wrong). Watching the failures pre-fix confirmed the tests were testing the *change* (Postgres URL → non-StaticPool engine), not pre-existing behaviour. TDD's RED-step working as advertised.
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before writing tests.** Verified: `tests/conftest.py:36-41` (pre-edit) had the hardcoded `create_engine` call; `app/db.py:9-15` has the parallel `_engine_kwargs` helper; `pyproject.toml:32` is `"psycopg[binary]>=3.2"` (the installed Postgres driver, not psycopg2); `app/audit.py::apply_immutability_triggers` already branches on `connection.dialect.name`; `app/config.py:21` defaults `database_url` to `"sqlite:///./dev.db"` (not in-memory).
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓ (auto-fix corrected one `I001` import-sort issue in `tests/conftest.py`); mypy ✓ (29 source files unchanged — CONFTEST1 is tests-only, mypy strict only on `app/`); pytest 2502 passed + 3 skipped (was 2492 + 3 at on-disk iter-74 HEAD `f452740`; **+10 CONFTEST1 tests**); e2e 16/16 passing (full re-run; tests-only refactor doesn't affect the e2e suite which has its own conftest at `tests/e2e/conftest.py`).

---

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — R5l:**

- **No DoD tick.** R5l extends DoD #7's already-ticked "Export any list view to CSV" coverage to the **thirteenth and final** surface; DoD #7 itself was ticked at iter-57 / D7. R5l is mechanical polish — a plus-one on the established pattern. **After R5l the R5 backlog item is fully closed** — every list view in the app has a CSV export. The next slice should target an unticked DoD: #1 (Google SSO + role assignment), #10 (zero-failure full suite), #11 (SQLite/Postgres parity), or #12-deploy.
- **URL-drift catch via cross-checking.** The "Next slice" prose in PROGRESS.md called the route `/admin/taxonomy/{parent_id}/sub`; the real route is `/admin/taxonomy/{parent_id}/children`. Caught by reading `app/taxonomy.py:545` before writing tests. The `/sub/` URL exists but only for per-sub-cat edit/archive/unarchive routes (`/admin/taxonomy/sub/{node_id}/...`), not the list view. Same lesson as DOC7's `/receive` first-run failure: anchor every promised URL substring to the actual decorator before writing the test.
- **CSV branch runs after the `_get_top_level_parent` guard.** Critical ordering: unknown parent → 404 and sub-cat parent (depth limit) → 400 happen *before* the CSV branch. If the order were reversed, an invalid parent_id would silently render an empty CSV (header row + no data) instead of a 4xx — confusing for the user and hiding the bad URL. Pinned by `test_unknown_parent_csv_is_404` + `test_sub_category_parent_csv_is_400`.
- **`show=archived` filter pinned by `body.count("Silver") == 1`.** The "Silver" / "Old Silver" archived-bucket assertion: when `show=archived` is selected, only "Old Silver" should appear, but "Old Silver" itself contains the substring "Silver" — so the test asserts `body.count("Silver") == 1` (a single mention) rather than `"Silver" not in body`. Slightly brittle to a future PR that adds a "Silver" header label or a "Silver" filter dropdown — that would push the count above 1 and trigger the assertion. Acceptable: any such addition would deserve scrutiny, and the test would fire as a forcing function. Same posture as the existing `test_list_show_archived` HTML test which uses an analogous `or "Old Silver"` disjunction trick.
- **Three columns is the right shape.** A combined "show level + parent_id" CSV with both top-level and sub-cat rows would either duplicate the existing top-level CSV or hide the data behind a `level` column. The per-parent-CSV shape preserves the natural URL hierarchy (CSV download lives at the same URL as the HTML page) and matches every prior CSV surface's "scope == route's scope" invariant. The parent context is in the filename (`subcategories_parent_5_active.csv`), not as a per-row column — every row in a given file shares the same parent so adding the column would be redundant.
- **`show` filter respected in the template link.** The HTML page's CSV link includes both `format=csv` and `show={{ show }}` so a Manager who clicks the "Archived" tab and then "Download CSV" gets the archived rows, not the active rows. Pinned by `test_html_renders_csv_link_with_archived_show`. Same posture as the top-level taxonomy CSV link (which also threads `show`).
- **No e2e walk for the CSV download itself.** The 21 integration tests pin every observable behaviour — Content-Type, Content-Disposition filename (with `parent_id` + `show` interpolation), body shape across empty / single-row / multi-row / show-active / show-archived / sort-order / cross-parent-isolation, role gates (6 cells), 404 + 400 guards, HTML fallthrough, audit invariance. The existing `test_taxonomy_e2e.py` walk was re-run and still passes (1/1). Adding a Playwright walk specifically for the CSV download would just verify the link renders + the GET succeeds, which the integration tests already cover. Same posture as the twelve prior CSV surfaces.
- **CSV-injection vulnerability still not addressed.** Carried forward from R5's self-critique. v1 posture: all-internal users (Manager + Admin only on this surface). Same posture as the prior 12 surfaces; a sanitiser pass can land once a real concern arises (e.g. once user-supplied `name` text — which a sub-category name is — could reach a CSV consumer who'd open it in Excel without the lib's safe-import path). A malicious Manager who creates a sub-category named `=cmd|...` would create a CSV-injection payload. The risk is low (Manager is already privileged) but non-zero; logged for the rolling self-critique.
- **First-run pass on 14 of 21 tests pre-implementation, 21 of 21 post-implementation.** The 14 pre-implementation passes were the role-enforcement matrix (the role gate is inherited by every method on the route, including the un-implemented CSV variants — which still went through the gate's 401/403 paths), the 404/400 guards (same `_get_top_level_parent` helper raises before any branch is reached), the `format=garbage` HTML fallthrough (pre-implementation the route ignored unknown `format` values entirely), and the `csv_writes_no_audit` test (no audit was being written before either — list view is read-only). The 7 failures (Headers + Body + Link tests) all fired with the right shape: `KeyError: 'content-disposition'`, `assert '<!doctype html...' == 'id,sort_order,name\r\n'`, `'data-testid="sub-csv-link"' not in body`. Watching the failures pre-implementation confirmed the tests were testing the *change*, not pre-existing behaviour — TDD's RED-step working as advertised.
- **Iter-73 count anchor was stale by 16 — re-anchored this iteration.** iter-73 PROGRESS.md "Current state" claimed `2471 passed + 3 skipped` but on-disk reality at iter-73 HEAD `feae48a` is 2487 + 3 (re-anchored via `git stash && uv run pytest --collect-only`). Same kind of count drift the iter-67 self-critique flagged about iter-66. The lesson keeps recurring: anchor counts to `pytest --collect-only` against the actual on-disk HEAD, never to PROGRESS.md prose recollection of prior counts. R5l's true delta is +21 (2487 → 2508), not +21 (2471 → 2492 implied by the stale anchor).
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before writing tests.** Verified: route at `app/taxonomy.py:545` was `@router.get("/{parent_id}/children", response_class=HTMLResponse)` (pre-edit) mounted at `/admin/taxonomy` (router prefix at `:56`); role gate `Depends(require_role(Role.MANAGER))` at `:550` (Admin via blanket override; Office + Workshop blocked); `_get_top_level_parent` at `:166-184` 404s on unknown id and 400s when parent is itself a sub-category; ordering `_LIST_ORDER, sort_order, name` at `:563`; same `archived_at` filter shape as the top-level `list_taxonomy` route at `:260-265`. Existing top-level `_TAXONOMY_CSV_HEADERS` + `_csv_rows_for_taxonomy` at `:229-246` were the structural template for `_SUB_CSV_HEADERS` + `_csv_rows_for_sub_categories`.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 source files unchanged), pytest 2508 passed + 3 skipped (was 2487 + 3 at on-disk iter-73 HEAD `feae48a`; **+21 R5l tests**), e2e 16/16 passing (`test_taxonomy_e2e.py` re-run individually + full e2e suite re-run; pure mechanical CSV-export extension, no UI behaviour change beyond the new download link).

---

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — R5k:**

- **No DoD tick.** R5k extends DoD #7's already-ticked "Export any list view to CSV" coverage to a twelfth surface; DoD #7 itself was ticked at iter-57 / D7. R5k is mechanical polish — a plus-one on the established pattern. After R5k only the sub-category list view (`/admin/taxonomy/{parent_id}/sub`) remains without CSV export — R5l if/when needed.
- **CSV ignores pagination — same posture as AC1's audit CSV.** The HTML branch keeps its 20-per-page pagination; the CSV branch returns every movement for the item. For an item with thousands of movements this loads them all in memory in one query — fine at v1 size (no item has come close to 1k movements), but worth noting as a future scaling concern. The fix when it bites is either a `?from=&to=` date-range filter (matches the COGS export's window-bounding) or chunked streaming via `StreamingResponse`. Logged for the rolling self-critique; defer until a real concern surfaces.
- **`_attach_directions_and_breakdown` does a breakdown query the CSV branch ignores.** Minor inefficiency — the helper attaches both `direction` (which the CSV needs) and `breakdown` (which the CSV builder ignores). Splitting into `_attach_directions(...)` + `_attach_breakdown(...)` would avoid the wasted query, but the cost is small (one indexed query keyed by movement id) and the helper is shared with the HTML render. Acceptable; if profiling later shows it as a bottleneck, the split is a one-method refactor.
- **Workshop sees the CSV download link.** Consistent with the existing HTML `item_detail` page, which already shows per-row `total_cost` to Workshop. Per MISSION §3 Workshop "Cannot see aggregated cost data or reports" — but per-item movement history isn't aggregation (it's the same data Workshop already sees on the page; CSV is just a different render). A stricter reading would say "make the CSV link Manager+Office only", but the HTML page is the precedent and the per-row total_cost is already exposed. No change.
- **Cost-layer breakdown deliberately not in the CSV.** The HTML timeline shows a sub-row under each OUT/negative-adjust movement with the layer-by-layer breakdown (`{qty} × {unit_cost} from layer received {ts}`). Including this in the CSV would mean either (a) a different shape — multiple rows per movement, with the parent row having its own row-id and child rows referencing it — that breaks the "one row per movement" mental model, or (b) JSON-encoding the breakdown into a single cell which is hostile to spreadsheet consumers. The right approach if the breakdown is needed is a separate `?include=breakdown` flag emitting two header sections, or a separate `/cost-layer-consumptions` route entirely. Defer.
- **`actor_email` is empty (not "—") for orphaned actors.** Same posture as AC1's audit CSV. The HTML uses `—` as a visual placeholder; the CSV uses an empty cell so spreadsheet sort behaves naturally (a `"—"` string would sort as text between alphabetical user emails). Pinned by `test_orphaned_actor_renders_empty_actor_email` which nullifies `movement.user_id` after the engine call to simulate a real orphan.
- **CSV-injection vulnerability not addressed.** Carried forward from R5's self-critique. v1 posture: all-internal users (Workshop-and-up on this surface). Same posture as the prior 11 surfaces; a sanitiser pass can land once a real concern arises (e.g. once user-supplied `reason` / `note` text could reach a CSV consumer who'd open it in Excel without the lib's safe-import path).
- **No e2e walk for the CSV download itself.** The 25 integration tests pin every observable behaviour — Content-Type, Content-Disposition filename (with `item_id` interpolation), body shape across IN / OUT / ADJUSTMENT-increase / ADJUSTMENT-decrease / TRANSFER, ordering newest-first, archived item still exports, all-rows-not-paginated, orphaned actor empty cell, role gates (6 cells), audit invariance. The existing `test_movements_e2e.py` walks were re-run and still pass (3/3). Adding a Playwright walk specifically for the CSV download would just verify the link renders + the GET succeeds, which the integration tests already cover. Same posture as the eleven prior CSV surfaces.
- **First-run pass on 24 of 25 tests.** One test (`test_movements_ordered_newest_first`) failed initially on a redundant sanity-check substring (`f",{first.id},"` doesn't match because the first column has no leading comma). The load-bearing assert (`ids = [int(row.split(",")[0]) for row in data_rows]`) was correct; dropped the redundant sanity check. After the one-line fix, all 25 pass. Logged as a reminder that "the first column has no leading comma" — a future R5 surface can pre-empt this gotcha by anchoring asserts on `row.split(",")` not on substring searches.
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before writing tests.** Verified: `_get_item_or_404` returns archived items (no archive check in helper at `app/movements.py:216`); `_PAGE_SIZE=20` (not 50 as my plan-stage memory had it); `_attach_directions_and_breakdown` runs two queries and attaches both `direction` and `breakdown` per movement; `MovementType.value` is lowercase (`"in"` / `"out"` / `"adjustment"` / `"transfer"`); `Numeric(14, 4)` columns round-trip Decimals at 4-decimal precision (`Decimal("10")` → `Decimal("10.0000")` → CSV cell `"10.0000"`); cost engine sets `movement.total_cost` exactly once per call (so `qty × unit_cost` for receipts, `Σ(take × layer.unit_cost)` for consumes).
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 source files unchanged), pytest 2471 passed + 3 skipped (was 2446 + 3 at iter-72 HEAD `6b9ac21`; **+25**), e2e 16/16 passing (`test_movements_e2e.py` + `test_items_e2e.py` re-run individually + full e2e suite re-run; pure mechanical CSV-export extension, no UI behaviour change beyond the new download link).

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — R5j:**

- **No DoD tick.** R5j extends DoD #7's already-ticked "Export any list view to CSV" coverage to an eleventh surface; DoD #7 itself was ticked at iter-57 / D7. R5j is mechanical polish — a plus-one on the established pattern. After R5j two v1 list views remain without CSV export: the per-item movements timeline (`/admin/items/{id}/movements`) and the sub-category list (`/admin/taxonomy/{parent_id}/sub`). Each is its own future R5k/l slice if/when needed; neither currently load-bears on a DoD.
- **`supplier_archived` rendered as `True`/`False` strings via Python `bool` → `str` coercion.** The CSV cell carries `True` or `False` (capitalised — Python's default `str(bool)` form). A spreadsheet import would interpret these as text strings, not booleans. Acceptable for v1 since the column is informational (the `(archived)` suffix in `supplier_name` is the load-bearing visual signal) and `csv_response` doesn't have a per-cell boolean coercion hook. A future polish slice could pre-coerce booleans to `"yes"`/`"no"` (matching the csv_export module's docstring example) but that would diverge from the established posture across other CSV surfaces — none of the prior 10 surfaces emit boolean cells, so this is the first instance of the policy decision. Logged for the rolling self-critique; defer until a real consumer complains.
- **Flattened-not-grouped CSV shape is the deliberate posture.** The HTML groups items by supplier into sections; the CSV emits one row per item with the supplier-grouping context as columns. A grouped CSV (one section per supplier with blank rows between, matching the HTML's visual structure) would be hostile to downstream filtering / pivoting in spreadsheet tools — most BI / spreadsheet tools expect flat tabular data. Same posture as R5b's PO list which flattens line items per PO into separate rows with the PO-id column repeated.
- **CSV link visible even on empty state — pinned by separate test.** `test_csv_link_visible_even_on_empty_state` asserts both `reorder-empty` and `reorder-csv-link` are present together. The empty-state CSV is header-only (just the header line + CRLF, no data rows) which is a useful "we ran the report and got nothing" artefact for a Manager auditing the empty state. Same posture as `csv_response`'s docstring: "header-only CSV is still a useful artefact".
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before writing tests.** Carried forward DOC4-R5i's "did I just promise something that doesn't exist?" lesson. Specifically verified: route at `app/reorder.py:111` was `@router.get("", response_class=HTMLResponse)` (pre-edit) mounted at `/admin/reorder` (router prefix at `:44`); role gate `Depends(require_role(Role.MANAGER, Role.OFFICE))` at `:114`; `_build_groups` returns groups dicts with `supplier_id` (or `None`), `supplier_label` (or `(no supplier)` constant at `:47`, or `<name> (archived)` suffix at `:84`), `supplier_archived` boolean, and `rows` list with `item` + `current_qty` + `threshold` + `reorder_qty` + `deficit`; HTML grouping order is suppliers alphabetically then no-supplier last (`:105-108`); `Item.current_qty` / `reorder_threshold` / `reorder_qty` are `Numeric(14, 4)` at `app/models.py:410-418` so a `Decimal("3")` round-trips as `Decimal("3.0000")` when read back from SQLAlchemy.
- **First-run pass on all 19 forcing-function tests.** No iteration needed — the pre-write cross-checking against source caught every gotcha before they could surface as test failures (e.g. the `Numeric(14, 4)` precision question that would have made `cells[7] == "3"` fail if I'd guessed). The pattern is now mechanical enough that a 19-test addition lands clean on first attempt.
- **CSV-injection vulnerability not addressed.** Carried forward from R5's self-critique — the `csv_export.py` module's docstring acknowledges this. v1 posture: all-internal users (Manager + Office on this surface). Same posture as the prior 10 surfaces; a sanitiser pass can land once a real concern arises (e.g. once user-supplied content from an untrusted source flows into a cell). The reorder dashboard's CSV cells are item SKU / item name / supplier name — all admin-controlled. Workshop can't get to this surface (role-gated 403).
- **No e2e walk for the CSV download itself.** The integration tests pin every observable behaviour — Content-Type, Content-Disposition filename, body content (across 5 distinct shape tests), role gates (6 cells), audit invariance, ordering. The existing `test_reorder_e2e.py` walk was re-run and still passes (1/1) — it exercises the full draft-PO chain end-to-end. Adding a Playwright walk specifically for the CSV download would just verify the link renders + the GET succeeds, which the integration tests already cover. Same posture as the ten prior CSV surfaces.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 source files unchanged), pytest 2446 passed + 3 skipped (was 2427 + 3 at iter-71 HEAD `279667a`; **+19**), e2e 16 passed (no count change; pure mechanical CSV-export extension, no UI behaviour change beyond the new download link).

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — R5i:**

- **No DoD tick.** R5i extends DoD #7's already-ticked "Export any list view to CSV" coverage to a tenth surface; DoD #7 itself was ticked at iter-57 / D7. R5i is mechanical polish — a plus-one on the established pattern. After R5i three v1 list views remain without CSV export: reorder dashboard, per-item movements timeline, and the sub-category list. Each is its own future R5j/k/l slice if/when needed; none currently load-bears on a DoD.
- **No `?show=archived` partition for users.** Unlike `locations` / `suppliers` / `taxonomy` which split active vs archived via `?show=` and encode the partition in the filename (`locations_active.csv` / `locations_archived.csv`), users have no archived state — there's only the `UserStatus` enum (`pending` / `active` / `disabled`). All three statuses live in the same view + same CSV. A future polish slice could add a `?status=` filter both to the HTML and the CSV (filename `users_active.csv` / `users_pending.csv` / etc.) but the current view's purpose is *managing* users (the admin needs to see pending users to act on them) so a default-everything view is right.
- **`created_at` carries full ISO datetime in CSV but only `%Y-%m-%d` in HTML.** Same minor inconsistency as the eight prior CSV surfaces. HTML formats user-friendly; CSV preserves precision via `csv_response`'s coercion. Documented inline in the helper docstring; downstream consumers benefit from the precision when sorting.
- **Empty `role` cell on pending users.** Pinned by `test_pending_user_has_empty_role_cell` and documented in the helper docstring. Spreadsheet receivers reading the CSV need to know the column can be empty — but this is the data, not a presentation issue. A null-as-string-`"none"` policy would lose information (a pending user *is* role-less, not role-`none`).
- **CSV-injection vulnerability not addressed.** Carried forward from R5's self-critique — the `csv_export.py` module's docstring acknowledges this. v1 posture: all-internal users (Admin-only here, even tighter than the prior surfaces). Same posture as the prior 9 surfaces; a sanitiser pass can land once a real concern arises (e.g. once user-supplied content from an untrusted source flows into a cell).
- **No e2e walk for the CSV download itself.** The integration tests pin every observable behaviour — Content-Type, Content-Disposition filename, body content, role gates, ordering, enum coercion, audit invariance. The existing `test_admin_users_e2e.py` walk was re-run and still passes (1/1) — it exercises the full HTML + role-promotion chain. Adding a Playwright walk specifically for the CSV download would just verify the link renders + the GET succeeds, which the integration tests already cover. Same posture as the nine prior CSV surfaces.
- **No "no users at all" body test (header-only emit).** The admin always exists in the user list (they're the requesting user — the route's session is theirs and they're returned by the `select(User)` query), so `len(rows) == 0` is impossible in practice. The plan originally included a test for that path but it would have required either bypassing auth or fabricating a query that excludes the requester — either of those would diverge from the actual route behaviour. Header-row presence is pinned by `test_header_row_is_first_line` instead, which is the same forcing function with realistic seeding.

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — DOC10:**

- **No DoD tick.** DOC10 closes the last two non-P4-gated `_TODO`s left in `README.md` (Contributing + License). DoD #12 still doesn't tick because the deploy section + deployed-URL Quick Link + Tech Stack deploy-target line are all gated on P4 (Fly.io vs Render decision + actual `fly.toml` / `render.yaml` config landing). After DOC10 every remaining README `_TODO` is *legitimately* waiting on un-shipped state — the file is no longer carrying stale placeholders for already-decided facts. P4 → DOC9-deploy-section → DoD #12 ticks.
- **License decision honoured `pyproject.toml`'s canonical declaration, not the Next-slice prose's MIT/Apache candidate.** The Next-slice section at PROGRESS.md:2932 floated "pick MIT or Apache-2.0 + add the SPDX identifier" but that conflicts with the existing `license = { text = "Proprietary" }` in `pyproject.toml` line 7. Silently swapping would either change the project's licence (a load-bearing legal scope change without authorization) or leave README + `pyproject.toml` disagreeing. Conservative move: match `pyproject.toml`. If the human reviewer wants the licence flipped to open-source, that goes through "Proposed scope changes" first. **Lesson**: Next-slice prose isn't authoritative — the canonical source files are. When the two disagree, defer to source + log the conflict.
- **4-way open-source absence pin (`MIT`/`Apache`/`GPL`/`BSD`) is the load-bearing forcing function.** A future PR that flips the licence must update `pyproject.toml` AND `README.md` AND `MISSION.md` AND `PROGRESS.md` "Proposed scope changes" before any one of those changes can land cleanly. The 4-way pin catches the most common open-source licence names; rare false-positives (e.g. prose like "unlike MIT-licensed projects, this is proprietary") would force a relaxation, which is an acceptable rare-cost trade-off for the strong forcing-function gain.
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before writing prose.** Carried forward DOC4-DOC9a's lesson: `pyproject.toml` line 7 is `license = { text = "Proprietary" }`; `MISSION.md` §10 is at lines 220-226; `MISSION.md` §8 is at lines 185-208; existing `## How this gets built` section at lines 394-409 already names the loop's flow + the human touch-points (so DOC10's Contributing section reinforces but doesn't duplicate); `loop.sh` exists at repo root. Forcing-function tests don't catch fictional surfaces by themselves — only reading the sections against actual source while writing it does.
- **First-run pass on all 8 forcing-function tests.** No iteration needed (continues DOC8 + DOC9a's first-run-pass streak). Suggests the cross-checking discipline before writing prose is stable; DOC10's plan was specific enough about every load-bearing token (`MISSION.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, `loop`, `pull request` OR `external`, `Proprietary`, `license = { text = "Proprietary" }`) that the first draft hit each forcing-function pin.
- **`_h2_section()` helper reuse continues.** DOC9a added it at module scope; DOC10 reuses it for both Contributing and License sections (no new helpers needed). The h2-vs-h3 split is now stable: workflow walks under `## Common workflows` are h3; top-level sections (Quick links, Tech stack, Contributing, License, eventually Deployment) are h2. A future P4-gated DOC9-deploy slice will reuse `_h2_section("Deployment")`.
- **Section length 1.0 KiB Contributing + 0.4 KiB License** — both above their respective floors (200 chars + 50 chars). License is intentionally short; an internal Proprietary tool doesn't warrant the multi-paragraph licence-text-or-link prose that an open-source project would carry. Compressing further would either drop the explicit no-rights-granted invariant (load-bearing legal copy) or drop the contact-UC-for-enquiries pointer (load-bearing user-facing call to action).
- **No e2e walk added.** Same posture as every prior DOC slice (1-9a). The on-disk file read is the right granularity for a docs artefact. The `_h2_section()` helper drives the same 8-test class structure as DOC9a's, and the existing 60+ unit + integration tests across `test_readme.py` cover every prior section's rendering. The Playwright suite is the right tool for UI behaviour; docs are read flat from disk.
- **Forcing-function shape now established at 9 sections × 4-8 tests each in `test_readme.py`** (5 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 8 + 8 + 4 + 8 = 68 tests). Single module is still readable; ~640 LoC + helpers. After P4-gated DOC9-deploy lands the file caps at ~75-80 tests — comfortably within "one module" boundaries.
- **Maintainability**: ~50 new LoC in the test file + ~150 words of prose. A competent dev can read + extend it in 5 min using DOC9a's section as a template. The two-pin (presence + absence) shape + the docs↔source consistency check + the absence pin against forbidden tokens are all reusable for future docs slices.
- **Engine isolation preserved.** No writers, no models, no router registration changes, no audit-trail edits. Pure README + tests addition. mypy source-file count stays at 29.
- **Repo health**: ruff ✓, mypy ✓ (29 source files unchanged), pytest 2426 passed + 3 skipped (was 2418 + 3 at iter-69 HEAD `f2e18d0`; **+8 DOC10 forcing-function tests**), e2e 16 passed (not re-run; DOC10 is pure README + tests addition).

**Self-critique notes (rolling) — DOC9a:**

- **No DoD tick.** DOC9a closes the two non-gated `_TODO` blocks left in `README.md` after DOC8 (Quick Links → changelog + Tech Stack → PDF library). DoD #12 still doesn't tick because the deploy section + the deployed-URL Quick Link + the deploy-target Tech Stack line are all gated on P4 (Fly.io vs Render decision), and the Contributing + License footer placeholders are gated on DOC10. After DOC9a, every remaining `_TODO` in the README is *legitimately* waiting on un-shipped state — the file is no longer carrying stale placeholders for already-decided facts.
- **Cross-checked every load-bearing claim against source before committing.** Carried forward DOC4-DOC8's lesson: `CHANGELOG.md` exists at repo root with first line `# Changelog` and a populated `## Unreleased` section; `pyproject.toml` line 32 is `"reportlab>=4.5.0"`; no `weasyprint` substring anywhere in `pyproject.toml`; `app/pdf.py` exists (the PO3 renderer); MISSION §5 says "WeasyPrint or reportlab. Pick one and stick with it." → DOC9a's resolution honours both halves. The `pyproject.toml` consistency check is a forcing function for future-DOC9a-style updates: if the PDF library is ever swapped, the README test fires + a docs update is required on the same PR.
- **Two-pin (presence + absence) test shape catches both forward-direction regressions.** Pinning *only* the presence of `[Changelog](./CHANGELOG.md)` would let a future PR add the link without removing the placeholder; pinning *only* the absence of `_TODO: changelog` would let a future PR drop the link entirely. The two-pin shape catches both — same posture as DOC7's two-test pin on `partially_received` (precondition vs outcome).
- **Cross-source consistency check (`test_pdf_choice_matches_pyproject`).** Reads `pyproject.toml` from disk + asserts `reportlab` present + `weasyprint` absent. Forces a docs update on a future PDF-library swap (the README would no longer match `pyproject.toml`). Same docs ↔ source consistency-pinning posture as A2's `test_audit_coverage.py` cross-cutting source-text sweep but applied at the docs ↔ deps boundary instead of the docs ↔ routes boundary. First time this style of check has appeared in `test_readme.py`; if a future docs slice surfaces another `pyproject.toml` claim (e.g. naming the deploy target), the same shape can be reused.
- **`_h2_section()` helper added at module scope.** Mirrors the existing `_section()` helper at line 22 but slices on `## ` (h2) instead of `### ` (h3). Reused by 2 of the 4 DOC9a tests (the changelog link + the PDF library tests both read top-level sections, not the `### `-nested workflow walks). Available for future docs slices that pin top-level sections (e.g. `## Tech stack`, `## Deployment`, `## Configuration`). DOC10's Contributing + License footer tests will use this helper too.
- **First-run pass on all 4 forcing-function tests.** No iteration needed (unlike DOC7's `/receive` first-run failure). The cross-checking discipline before writing prose continues to be stable: DOC9a's plan was specific enough about every load-bearing token that the first draft hit each forcing-function pin.
- **No e2e walk added.** Same posture as DOC1-DOC8. The on-disk file read is the right granularity for a docs artefact. The four tests run in <0.1s as part of `make test` — adding an e2e walk would be over-engineering for a 2-line README edit.
- **Maintainability**: ~30 new LoC for the `TestQuickLinksAndTechStackTodoResolution` class + `_h2_section()` helper. A competent dev can read + extend it in 5 min using DOC8's section as a template. The forcing-function shape is now a stable template across 9 sections.
- **Engine isolation preserved.** No writers, no models, no router registration changes, no audit-trail behaviour edits. Pure README + tests addition. mypy source-file count stays at 29.
